'''Mario Gabriele Andretti''' (born February 28, 1940) is an American <!-- Per [[MOS:ETHNICITY]], please do NOT add "Italian" to the lede. -->former [[racing driver]] who competed in [[Formula One]] from {{F1|1968}} to {{F1|1982}}, and [[American open-wheel racing|IndyCar]] from [[1964 USAC Championship Car season|1964]] to [[1994 IndyCar season|1994]]. Andretti won the [[Formula One World Drivers' Championship]] in {{F1|1978}} with [[Team Lotus|Lotus]], and won 12 [[Formula One Grands Prix|Grands Prix]] across 14 seasons. In [[American open-wheel racing]], Andretti won four [[List of American open-wheel racing national champions|IndyCar National Championship titles]] and the [[Indianapolis 500]] in [[1969 Indianapolis 500|1969]]; in [[stock car racing]], he won the [[Daytona 500]] in [[1967 Daytona 500|1967]]. In [[Endurance racing (motorsport)|endurance racing]], Andretti is a three-time winner of the [[12 Hours of Sebring]].

Andretti and his family were displaced from Italian-controlled [[Istria]] during the [[Istrian–Dalmatian exodus]] and eventually emigrated to [[Nazareth, Pennsylvania]] in 1955. He began [[dirt track racing]] with his twin brother [[Aldo Andretti|Aldo]] four years later, with Andretti progressing to [[USAC Championship Car]] in [[1964 USAC Championship Car season|1964]]. In [[American open-wheel racing|open-wheel racing]], he won back-to-back [[United States Auto Club|USAC]] titles in [[1965 USAC Championship Car season|1965]] and [[1966 USAC Championship Car season|1966]], also finishing runner-up in [[1967 USAC Championship Car season|1967]] and [[1968 USAC Championship Car season|1968]]. He also contested [[stock car racing]] in his early career, winning the [[1967 Daytona 500]] with [[Holman-Moody]]. He took his first major [[sportscar racing]] victory at the [[1967 12 Hours of Sebring|12 Hours of Sebring]] that year with [[Ford Motor Company|Ford]]. Andretti debuted in Formula One at the {{F1GP|1968|United States}} in {{F1|1968}} with [[Team Lotus|Lotus]], where he qualified on [[List of Formula One polesitters|pole position]]. He contested several further Grands Prix with Lotus in {{F1|1969}}, when he won his [[1969 USAC Championship Car season|third USAC title]] and the [[1969 Indianapolis 500|Indianapolis 500]]. In {{F1|1970}}, Andretti took his maiden podium finish at the {{F1GP|1970|Spanish}} with [[STP (motor oil company)|STP]], driving a [[privateer (motorsport)|privateer]] [[March 701]]. He signed for [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]] that year, winning [[1970 12 Hours of Sebring|at Sebring]] again.

Andretti took his maiden victory in Formula One at the season-opening {{F1GP|1971|South African}} in {{F1|1971}}, on debut for Ferrari. He took his third Sebring victory the [[1972 12 Hours of Sebring|following year]]. After part-time roles for Ferrari and [[Parnelli]] in {{F1|1972}} and {{F1|1974}}, respectively, Andretti joined the latter full-time for {{F1|1975}} after finishing runner-up in the [[1974 SCCA/USAC Formula 5000 Championship|SCCA Continental Championship]]. He moved back to Lotus in {{F1|1976}}, winning the season-ending {{F1GP|1976|Japanese}} and helping develop the [[Lotus 78|78]]. Andretti won four Grands Prix in {{F1|1977}}, finishing third in the [[World Drivers' Championship]]. He won the title in {{F1|1978}} after achieving six victories, becoming the [[Formula One drivers from the United States|second World Drivers' Champion from the United States]]. After winless {{F1|1979}} and {{F1|1980}} campaigns with Lotus, he moved to [[Alfa Romeo in Formula One|Alfa Romeo]] in {{F1|1981}}. Following two fill-in appearances for [[Williams Racing|Williams]] and Ferrari in {{F1|1982}}, Andretti retired from Formula One with 12 wins, 18 pole positions, 10 fastest laps and 19 podiums.

Andretti returned to full-time IndyCar racing in [[1982 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1982]], placing third in the standings with [[Patrick Racing|Patrick]], amongst winning the [[1982 Michigan 500|Michigan 500]]. After finishing third again with [[Newman/Haas Racing|Newman/Haas]] in his [[1983 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1983]] campaign, he won his fourth IndyCar title in [[1984 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1984]], 15 years after the previous and his first sanctioned by [[Championship Auto Racing Teams|CART]]. He won the [[1986 Pocono 500|Pocono 500]] in [[1986 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1986]] and remained with Newman/Haas until [[1994 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1994]]; his victory at [[Jimmy Bryan Memorial|Phoenix]] in [[1993 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1993]] made him the oldest winner in IndyCar history, aged 53, as well as the first driver to win a race in four different decades. Andretti retired with 52 wins, 65 pole positions, and 141 podiums in IndyCar. His 111 official victories on major circuits across several motorsport disciplines saw his name become synonymous with speed in American popular culture.{{efn|Alternatively given as 109.}} His sons, [[Michael Andretti|Michael]] and [[Jeff Andretti|Jeff]], were both racing drivers, the former winning the CART title in [[1991 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1991]] and previously owning [[Andretti Global]]. Andretti serves on the board of directors of [[Cadillac in Formula One|Cadillac]] in Formula One from its debut in {{F1|2026}} onwards. He was inducted into the [[International Motorsports Hall of Fame]] in 2000.

== Early life ==
=== Childhood in Italy ===
Mario Gabriele Andretti was born on February 28, 1940,{{efn|One source lists Andretti's birthday as [[February 29]], that is, Leap Day.<ref name="NSCHoF" /> However, Andretti's official naturalization certificate lists his birthday as February 28.<ref name="Naturalization" />}} to an [[Istrian Italians|Istrian-Italian]] family in [[Montona d'Istria|Montona]], Istria, Kingdom of Italy (present-day Motovun, Croatia).<ref name="Personal life">{{Cite web |title=Up Close and Personal |url=https://www.marioandretti.com/personal |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=MarioAndretti.com |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194910/https://www.marioandretti.com/personal |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="sportscentury" /> He was born six hours before his twin brother [[Aldo Andretti|Aldo]].<ref name="Comiskey–2017">{{Cite web |last=Comiskey |first=Daniel S. |date=2017-05-02 |title=The Wrecked, Rebuilt Life Of Aldo Andretti |url=https://www.indianapolismonthly.com/arts-and-culture/sports/the-wrecked-rebuilt-life-of-aldo-andretti |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=Indianapolis Monthly |language=en-US |archive-date=January 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250126213600/https://www.indianapolismonthly.com/arts-and-culture/sports/the-wrecked-rebuilt-life-of-aldo-andretti |url-status=live }}</ref> He is the son of Alvise "Gigi" Andretti, who worked as a farm administrator in Italy and for [[Bethlehem Steel]] in the U.S.,<ref>{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=1999-11-03 |title=Alvise 'Gigi' Andretti |url=https://www.mcall.com/1999/11/03/alvise-gigi-andretti/ |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=[[The Morning Call]] |language=en-US |archive-date=April 12, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250412233335/https://www.mcall.com/1999/11/03/alvise-gigi-andretti/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Reinhard–2016">{{Cite web |last=Reinhard |first=Paul |date=2016-02-18 |title=Mario Andretti gets honorary citizenship in hometown |url=https://www.mcall.com/2016/02/17/mario-andretti-gets-honorary-citizenship-in-hometown/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=[[The Morning Call]] |language=en-US}}</ref> and his wife Rina.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2003-12-30 |title=Rina Andretti |url=https://www.mcall.com/obituaries/rina-andretti-pa/ |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=[[The Morning Call]] |language=en-US}}</ref> He also had an older sister, Anna Maria Andretti Burley.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Anna Burley Obituary (1934 - 2018) - Melbourne, FL - FloridaToday |url=https://www.legacy.com/us/obituaries/floridatoday/name/anna-burley-obituary?id=12103197 |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=Legacy.com}}</ref>

Andretti's family owned a {{convert|2100|acre|adj=on}} farm in Montona,<ref name="Andretti–2016">{{Cite web |last=Andretti |first=Mario |date=2016-06-09 |title=Letter to My Younger Self |url=https://www.theplayerstribune.com/articles/2016-6-9-mario-andretti-racing-letter-to-my-younger-self |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=The Players' Tribune |language=en-US |archive-date=January 16, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250116055119/https://www.theplayerstribune.com/articles/2016-6-9-mario-andretti-racing-letter-to-my-younger-self |url-status=live }}</ref> but after World War II, the [[Paris Peace Treaties, 1947|Treaty of Paris]] (1947) transferred the territory to [[Communist Party of Yugoslavia|communist]]-controlled [[Yugoslavia]]. As a result, the Andretti family joined the [[Istrian–Dalmatian exodus]] in 1948. The family lost all their land and was permitted to take only one truckload of possessions.<ref name="Andretti–2016" /> They spent seven years in a refugee camp in [[Lucca]],<ref name="Lawrence–2015">{{Cite magazine |last=Lawrence |first=Andrew |date=2015-05-19 |title=After winning racing's rarest trifecta, Mario Andretti's ventures are thriving |url=https://www.si.com/racing/2015/05/19/mario-andretti-petroleum-winery-profiles |access-date=2025-01-26 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en-US |archive-date=July 30, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210730145145/https://www.si.com/racing/2015/05/19/mario-andretti-petroleum-winery-profiles |url-status=live }}</ref> living in an abandoned college dormitory without running water.<ref name="Reinhard–2016" />

The Andretti twins were interested in racing at an early age. At age five, they raced hand-crafted wooden cars through the Montona streets.<ref name="milwaukee">{{cite web |author=Garu D'Amato |date=August 2, 2003 |title=Appreciating Andretti |url=http://www.findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_qn4196/is_20030802/ai_n10897303 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130726105951/http://www.findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_qn4196/is_20030802/ai_n10897303 |archive-date=July 26, 2013 |access-date=April 12, 2007 |work=[[Milwaukee Journal Sentinel]]}} {{Dead link|date=January 2025}}</ref> After moving to Lucca, the brothers got a job parking cars at a local garage.<ref name="Gushue–2016">{{cite web |last=Gushue |first=Ted |date=2016-03-01 |title=Mario Andretti On Enzo Ferrari, Colin Chapman, And Growing Up On The Race Track |url=https://petrolicious.com/articles/mario-andretti-on-enzo-ferrari-colin-chapman-and-growing-up-on-the-race-track |access-date=2022-08-02 |work=Petrolicious |publisher=[[duPont Registry]] |archive-date=August 12, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220812052345/https://petrolicious.com/articles/mario-andretti-on-enzo-ferrari-colin-chapman-and-growing-up-on-the-race-track |url-status=live }}</ref> In his autobiography, Andretti wrote, "The first time I fired up a car, felt the engine shudder and the wheel come to life in my hands, I was hooked. It was a feeling I can't describe. I still get it every time I get into a race car."<ref name="Bantam–1970">{{Cite book |last1=Andretti |first1=Mario |url= |title=What's It Like Out There? |last2=Collins |first2=Bob |publisher=Bantam |year=1970 |isbn=978-0-8092-9672-9 |location=New York}}</ref>{{rp||page=6}}

The garage owners noticed the brothers' passion for racing and brought them to watch the [[1954 Mille Miglia]], which was won by two-time Formula One champion [[Alberto Ascari]].<ref name="Gushue–2016" /><ref name="mshf">{{cite web |title=Biography |url=http://www.mshf.com/index.htm?/hof/andretti_mario.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070928024812/http://www.mshf.com/index.htm?%2Fhof%2Fandretti_mario.htm |archive-date=September 28, 2007 |access-date=February 22, 2007 |work=[[Motorsports Hall of Fame of America]]}}</ref> Ascari became Andretti's personal idol.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Blackstock |first=Elizabeth |date=2025-01-19 |title=Mario Andretti: The American racer who rocked the world of F1 |url=https://www.planetf1.com/features/mario-andretti-the-american-racer-who-rocked-the-world-of-f1 |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=PlanetF1 |language=en |archive-date=January 19, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250119200804/https://www.planetf1.com/features/mario-andretti-the-american-racer-who-rocked-the-world-of-f1 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Andretti–2015">{{Cite web |last=Andretti |first=Mario |date=8 September 2015 |title=Mario Andretti and the Brutal Magic of Monza |url=https://www.thedrive.com/article/195/mario-andretti-and-the-brutal-magic-of-monza |access-date=9 April 2023 |website=The Drive |archive-date=June 3, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230603073845/https://www.thedrive.com/article/195/mario-andretti-and-the-brutal-magic-of-monza |url-status=live }}</ref> The twins also visited [[Autodromo Nazionale Monza|Monza]] for the [[1954 Italian Grand Prix|Italian Grand Prix]], where Andretti saw Ascari race against [[Juan Manuel Fangio]].<ref name="Andretti–2015" /> Although the twins did not have a grandstand seat, Andretti recalled "being just mesmerized, overwhelmed by the sound, by the speed."<ref name="Gushue–2016" />

=== Move to the United States ===
Following a three-year wait for U.S. visas, the Andretti family moved to the United States in 1955. After an eleven-day journey on the [[SS Conte Biancamano|SS ''Conte Biancamano'']], they sailed into [[New York Harbor]] on Anna Maria's birthday of June 16.<ref name="Comiskey–2017" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Hembree |first=Mike |date=2017-06-09 |title=Mario Andretti: Racing royalty has no intention of slowing down |url=https://www.autoweek.com/racing/indycar/a1823151/mario-andretti-racing-royalty-has-no-intention-slowing-down/ |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=[[Autoweek]] |language=en-US |archive-date=January 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250126204826/https://www.autoweek.com/racing/indycar/a1823151/mario-andretti-racing-royalty-has-no-intention-slowing-down/ |url-status=live }}</ref> With just $125 in cash, they settled in [[Nazareth, Pennsylvania]], where Alvise Andretti's brother-in-law Tony lived. Although Alvise planned to leave after five years, the family never left the United States.<ref name="Andretti–2016" />

Andretti opposed leaving Italy at the time.<ref name="Andretti–2016" /> His father felt that moving to America would give his children the best opportunity to succeed in life,<ref name="auto">{{cite book |author=Stenning, Paul |author-link=Paul Stenning |title=Success - By Those Who've Made It. Pg.25 |date=November 24, 2013 |publisher=In Flight Books |isbn=978-1628475869}}</ref> but did not want his sons to become motor racers, as the sport was extremely dangerous at the time.<ref name="Andretti–2016" /> Andretti planned to become a welder,<ref name="ESPNclassic">{{cite web |last=Acocella |first=Nick |date=2003-11-19 |title=Andretti's only Indy 500 win came in 1969 |url=https://www.espn.com/classic/s/add_andretti_mario.html |access-date=April 21, 2007 |work=ESPN |archive-date=July 4, 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170704124613/http://www.espn.com/classic/s/add_andretti_mario.html |url-status=live }}</ref> but racing was "the only passion [he] really had career wise," and he admitted that he might not have been able to become a racer if he had stayed in Italy.<ref name="auto" /> Andretti's father did not watch him race until Andretti reached IndyCar in 1964.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Slon |first=Steven |date=2017-05-23 |title=Why Mario Andretti Is the Greatest Race Car Driver of All Time |url=https://www.saturdayeveningpost.com/2017/05/mario-andretti-young-immigrant-dream/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=The Saturday Evening Post |language=en-US |archive-date=March 23, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250323051056/https://www.saturdayeveningpost.com/2017/05/mario-andretti-young-immigrant-dream/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

In his 1970 biography, Andretti said that he became a [[naturalization|naturalized]] U.S. citizen on April 15, 1964.<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=xiii}} (His IndyCar debut was April 19, 1964.<ref name="ESPN" />) Andretti later revealed that he actually obtained U.S. citizenship on April 7, 1965.<ref name="Naturalization">{{Cite web |last=Andretti |first=Mario |date=2024-04-07 |title="59 years ago today on April 7, 1965, I became a Naturalized Citizen..." |url=https://x.com/MarioAndretti/status/1776986690516648409 |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=Twitter |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194955/https://x.com/MarioAndretti/status/1776986690516648409 |url-status=live }}</ref>

== Early racing career ==
=== Debut in dirt track racing ===
[[File:Mario and aldo.jpg|thumb|Mario (left) and his brother [[Aldo Andretti|Aldo]] at the [[2007 Indianapolis 500]] pole day|left]]The first car Andretti regularly drove was his father's 1957 Chevrolet, which the twins did not race, but nonetheless upgraded with features like a [[glasspack]] muffler and fuel injection.<ref name="Gushue–2016" /> The twins were surprised to find that Nazareth hosted a half-mile [[Dirt track racing|dirt track]], [[Nazareth Speedway]].<ref name="sportscentury" /> They used money they made working at their uncle's [[Sunoco]] station<ref name="Andretti–2016" /> to refurbish a 1948 [[Hudson Super Six|Hudson]],<ref name="sportscentury" /> using a stolen beer barrel as a fuel tank.<ref name="Andretti–2016" /> The car was ready to race when the twins were 19 years old, but the minimum age to race was 21, so the brothers convinced a newspaper editor to falsify their drivers' licenses.<ref name="Andretti–2016" /> After Aldo got into a major accident, the local chief of police spotted the forgery but turned a blind eye to save Aldo's health insurance.<ref name="Gushue–2016" />

The twins did not tell their father that they were racing until Aldo fractured his skull in a race and spent 62 days in a coma. Andretti's father nearly disowned Mario when the latter insisted on racing again, but eventually relented. Aldo also resumed racing, but suffered a career-ending accident in 1969.<ref name="Andretti–2016" />

The twins got off to a good start, picking up two wins each in [[NASCAR Sportsman Division|sportsman racing]] after their first four races.<ref name="ESPN">{{cite web | author=Schwartz, Larry | title=Mario Andretti synonymous with racing | publisher=[[ESPN]] | url=https://www.espn.com/sportscentury/features/00014066.html | access-date=February 22, 2007 | archive-date=August 9, 2017 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170809043820/http://www.espn.com/sportscentury/features/00014066.html | url-status=live }}</ref> In their first two weeks of racing, they won $300; they had previously been making $45 a week at the gas station.<ref name="Andretti–2016" /> From 1960 to 1961, Mario won 21 out of 46 [[Modified racing|modified stock car]] races.<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=37}} The twins raced against each other only once, at [[Oswego Speedway]] in 1967; Mario won, with Aldo finishing tenth after a brake failure.<ref name="Comiskey–2017" />

To intimidate their opponents, the twins bought Italian racing suits and fabricated a story about racing in junior formulae back in Italy. Andretti maintained the fiction for many years.<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=|pages=10–11}}<ref name="texaco">{{cite web |title=Texaco/Havoline CART History Mario Andretti |url=http://www.texacohavoline42.com/media/history/cart/mario_andretti.asp |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20061222035257/http://www.texacohavoline42.com/media/history/cart/mario_andretti.asp |archive-date=December 22, 2006 |access-date=April 12, 2007 |work=[[Texaco]]/[[Havoline]]}}</ref><ref name="NSCHoF">{{cite web |title=Mario Andretti |url=http://www.sprintcarhof.com/FileGet.aspx?ID=144 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070807075906/http://www.sprintcarhof.com/FileGet.aspx?ID=144 |archive-date=August 7, 2007 |access-date=2025-01-26 |publisher=National Sprint Car Hall of Fame}}</ref> In 2016, he admitted that the story was fabricated. He recalled that it "psych[ed] [the opponents] out, big time."<ref name="Andretti–2016" />

=== Single-seater racing ===
Despite his early successes in modified stock cars, Andretti's goal was to race in single-seater [[Open wheel racing|open-wheel]] cars.<ref name=NSCHoF /> He started by racing [[midget car racing|midget cars]] in the [[American Racing Drivers Club]] (ARDC) series from 1961 to 1963, starting with 3/4 (sized) midgets before graduating to full-sized midgets.<ref name="NSCHoF" /> In March 1962, he won a midget race, which he dubbed "my first victory of any consequence."<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=45}} He raced in over one hundred events in 1963,<ref name="8W">{{cite web |author=Diepraam |first1=Mattijs |last2=Nyberg |first2=Rainer |date=2004-12-16 |title=The Champions: Mario Andretti |url=http://www.forix.com/8w/andretti.html |access-date=June 14, 2007 |work=Forix |publisher=[[Motorsport Network]] |archive-date=September 28, 2007 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070928044242/http://www.forix.com/8w/andretti.html |url-status=live }}</ref> and scored 29 top-five finishes in 46 ARDC races.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015">{{Cite web |last=Malsher-Lopez |first=David |date=2015-11-09 |title=Mario Andretti's Career Turning Point |url=https://www.motorsport.com/indycar/news/mario-andretti-s-career-turning-point/656122/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=[[Motorsport.com]] |publisher=[[Motorsport Network]] |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194555/https://www.motorsport.com/indycar/news/mario-andretti-s-career-turning-point/656122/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He finished third in the 1963 ARDC season standings.<ref name="O'Leary–2002">{{Cite book |last=O'Leary |first=Mike |url= |title=Mario Andretti |publisher=MBI Pub Co. |year=2002 |isbn=978-0-7603-1399-2 |location=St. Paul, MN}}</ref>{{rp||page=17}} On [[Labor Day]] in 1963, Andretti won three feature races at two different tracks, an afternoon race at [[Flemington, New Jersey|Flemington]] and a doubleheader at [[Hatfield, Pennsylvania|Hatfield]], after which reporter [[Chris Economaki]] told him that "you just bought the ticket to the big time."<ref name="NSCHoF" />

From midget cars, the next step on the East Coast racing ladder was [[sprint car racing]], first with the United Racing Club (URC) series and then with the [[United States Auto Club]] (USAC) series. Andretti attempted to secure a full-time URC ride, but received only spot starts. However, USAC team owner Rufus Gray gave him a full-time drive for 1964.<ref name="NSCHoF" /> He won one race at [[Salem Speedway|Salem]] and finished third in the season standings behind veterans [[Don Branson]] and [[Jud Larson]].<ref name="USAC National Sprint Car Feature Winners">{{Cite web |title=USAC National Sprint Car Feature Winners |url=https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/676c9c9281060ef0c7154121_USAC%20Sprint%20Winners%20List.pdf |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=United States Auto Club |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194921/https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/676c9c9281060ef0c7154121_USAC%20Sprint%20Winners%20List.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="USAC National Sprint Car Standings">{{Cite web |title=USAC National Sprint Car Standings |url=https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/676c9c999819d58320b51dc2_USAC%20Sprint%20Standings.pdf |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=United States Auto Club}}</ref> To cover his expenses, he worked as a foreman at a golf cart factory.<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=60}}

Andretti continued to race in sprint cars after progressing to IndyCar. In 1965 he won once at [[Ascot Park (speedway)|Ascot Park]],<ref name="USAC National Sprint Car Feature Winners" /> and finished tenth in the season standings.<ref name="USAC National Sprint Car Standings" /> In 1966 he won five times ([[Cumberland, Maryland|Cumberland]], [[Oswego Speedway|Oswego]], [[Eldora Speedway|Rossburg]], Salem, and [[Manzanita Speedway|Phoenix]]),<ref name="USAC National Sprint Car Feature Winners" /> but finished second in the standings, behind [[Roger McCluskey]].<ref name="USAC National Sprint Car Standings" /> In 1967 he won two of the three events that he entered.<ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=26}}

== USAC IndyCar career ==
From 1956 to 1978, the top [[open-wheel racing]] series in North America was the [[USAC National Championship]], alternatively referred to as [[IndyCar]] or [[Champ cars|Champ Car]].<ref name="IndyCar split and reunification">{{Cite web |last=Blackstock |first=Elizabeth |date=2024-08-03 |title=Explained: The chaotic history of the IndyCar split and reunification |url=https://www.planetf1.com/features/indycar-split-reunification-history-explained |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=PlanetF1 |language=en |archive-date=February 1, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250201110331/https://www.planetf1.com/features/indycar-split-reunification-history-explained |url-status=live }}</ref> In [[1971 USAC Championship Car season|1971]], USAC split off its dirt-track races into a separate National Dirt Car Championship. The pavement championship retained the name [[United States Auto Club#USAC Championship Car Series|USAC Championship Car Series]], while the dirt championship had fewer races and was later rebranded to the "[[United States Auto Club#USAC Silver Crown Series|Silver Crown Series]]."<ref>{{Cite web |last=Weaver |first=Matt |date=2019-06-07 |title=USAC SIlver Crown Hoosier Hundred runs for the last time |url=https://www.autoweek.com/racing/more-racing/a2139006/usac-silver-crown-hoosier-hundred-runs-last-time/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=[[Autoweek]] |language=en-US |archive-date=October 30, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201030091616/https://www.autoweek.com/racing/more-racing/a2139006/usac-silver-crown-hoosier-hundred-runs-last-time/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=50}}

=== Breaking in (1964) ===
Andretti entered IndyCar during the [[1964 USAC Championship Car season|1964]] season, while still racing full-time in sprint cars. On April 19, 1964, the Doug Stearly team gave him a spot start at the [[Trenton 150|1964 Trenton 100]].<ref name="ESPN" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=1964 Trenton 100 |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/race-results/1964_Trenton_100/UO/ |access-date=2025-02-01 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=August 10, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240810222106/https://www.racing-reference.info/race-results/1964_Trenton_100/UO/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He started 16th and finished 11th.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015" />

Andretti spent the first portion of the 1964 season trying to find a full-time IndyCar drive. An opening appeared to materialize when one of the big three IndyCar teams,<ref>{{Cite web |last=Pruett |first=Marshall |date=2016-05-23 |title=Indy Diaries: How Roger Penske accidentally gave Mario Andretti his start |url=https://racer.com/2016/05/23/indy-diaries-how-roger-penske-accidentally-gave-mario-andretti-his-start/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=[[Racer (magazine)|RACER]] |language=en-US |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194619/https://racer.com/2016/05/23/indy-diaries-how-roger-penske-accidentally-gave-mario-andretti-his-start/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Dean Van Lines Racing Division (DVL), lost [[Chuck Hulse]] to injury.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015" /> Andretti met with DVL's chief mechanic, Clint Brawner, to ask for the drive. Although Andretti had come with an introduction from his sprint car team owner, Rufus Gray, Brawner turned Andretti down, as he was skeptical of sprint car racing and felt that Andretti was not ready to compete.<ref name="NSCHoF" /><ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015" /> He hired [[Bob Mathouser]] to replace Hulse.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015" /> Andretti joined Lee Glessner's outfit, but was forced to sit out the [[1964 Indianapolis 500]].<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015" />

=== Dean Van Lines, Andretti Racing, and STP (1964–1971) ===
[[File:Mario Andretti en 1970.jpg|left|thumb|Andretti in 1970.]]Andretti got his big break with DVL midway through the 1964 season, after the youngster impressed Brawner in two races: a sprint car race in [[Terre Haute, Indiana]]<ref name="NSCHoF" /> and an IndyCar race at [[Langhorne Speedway]], where Andretti finished ninth, just three places below Mathouser, who had the better car.<ref>{{Cite web |title=1964 Langhorne 100 |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/race-results/1964_Langhorne_100/UO/ |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=September 25, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230925205041/https://www.racing-reference.info/race-results/1964_Langhorne_100/UO/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=|pages=78–80}} Brawner had mentored a young [[A. J. Foyt]], and noticed that Andretti "worked as diligently on the car as Foyt had as a rookie with me."<ref name="Indy 500 Mechanic">{{Cite book |last1=Brawner |first1=Clint |url= |title=Indy 500 Mechanic |last2=Scalzo |first2=Joe |publisher=Chilton Book Co. |year=1975 |isbn=978-0-8019-6064-2 |location=Radnor, PA}}</ref>{{rp||page=130|pages=}} Andretti was pleased to join what he called one of the "few outfits worth driving for."<ref>{{Cite web |last=Nye |first=Doug |date=1974 |title=A Meeting with Mario Andretti at Watkins Glen - 1974 (Part One) |url=https://www.racingarchives.org/blogpost/a-meeting-with-mario-andretti-at-watkins-glen-1974-part-one/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=International Motor Racing Research Center |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194702/https://www.racingarchives.org/blogpost/a-meeting-with-mario-andretti-at-watkins-glen-1974-part-one/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He completed the final eight races of the season with DVL,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1964 USAC Champ Car Series Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1964/UO/ |access-date=2025-02-01 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=October 5, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231005105424/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1964/UO/ |url-status=live }}</ref> finishing 11th in the season standings.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver/Mario_Andretti/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |work=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=January 29, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250129020555/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver/Mario_Andretti/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He was named [[IndyCar Rookie of the Year]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=USAC National Championship Rookies of the Year |url=https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/65fb202b0577154372636734_USAC%20Indy%20Car%20Rookies%20of%20the%20Year.pdf |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=United States Auto Club |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194528/https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/65fb202b0577154372636734_USAC%20Indy%20Car%20Rookies%20of%20the%20Year.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref> After the season, Brawner agreed to make Andretti his permanent driver in place of Hulse.<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=|pages=97–98}}

==== 1965–1969: Years of domination ====
The Andretti-Brawner combination would soon come to dominate the sport. It quickly attracted technical and financial support from [[Firestone Tire and Rubber Company|Firestone]] and [[Ford Motor Company|Ford]]; Brawner said that Ford treated DVL like a [[works team]].<ref name="Indy 500 Mechanic" />{{rp||page=|pages=150, 157}} From 1965 to 1969, Andretti won three USAC IndyCar titles. He also came within 93 points of winning five in a row; for comparison, at the time, 100 points was the difference between [[List of American Championship car racing points scoring systems#AAA Championships 1946–1955 and USAC Championships 1956–1977|finishing sixth and seventh]] at the Indianapolis 500. At the peak of his statistical dominance, Andretti won 29 of 85 USAC championship races between 1966 and 1969.<ref name="sportscentury" />

In [[1965 USAC Championship Car season|1965]], Andretti's first full season with DVL, he took advantage of the team's new [[Brawner Hawk]], a derivation of the [[Brabham]] Formula One chassis.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015" /> His third-place finish at the [[1965 Indianapolis 500]] earned him the race's [[List of Indianapolis 500 Rookies of the Year|Rookie of the Year]] award.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015" /> He won his first IndyCar race at the [[Hoosier Grand Prix]].<ref name="IMHoF">{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti |url=https://www.motorsportshalloffame.com/inductees/mario-andretti/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=International Motorsports Hall of Fame |language=en-US |archive-date=January 15, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250115080925/https://www.motorsportshalloffame.com/inductees/mario-andretti/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Although he won only one race that year, he scored six second places and three third places, and scored points in 16 out of 18 races.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1965 USAC Champ Car Series Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1965/UO/ |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=October 5, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231005005743/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1965/UO/ |url-status=live }}</ref> His closest competitor, A. J. Foyt (who had won four of the last five USAC titles) won five races but failed to score seven times.<ref>{{Cite web |title=A. J. Foyt: 1965 USAC Champ Car Series Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/foyta.01/1965/UO/ |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=May 7, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210507231627/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/foyta.01/1965/UO/ |url-status=live }}</ref> At age 25, Andretti became the youngest IndyCar champion in history,<ref name="8W" /> a record he held for thirty years until [[Jacques Villeneuve]] won the [[1995 PPG Indy Car World Series|1995]] title.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015" /> To his irritation, however, when he appeared on [[The Tonight Show Starring Johnny Carson|Johnny Carson]] at the end of the season, he was introduced as the Indy 500 Rookie of the Year, which he felt downplayed his title win.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015" />{{efn|Andretti had previously told a similar story about ''[[The Joey Bishop Show (talk show)|The Joey Bishop Show]]'',<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=|pages=xi-xii}} but that show premiered in 1967. Andretti may have appeared on both shows in the 1960s, though not necessarily at the end of the 1965 season.<ref>{{Cite web |title="Tonight Show starring Johnny Carson" (NBC) Season 7 (1968-69) |url=https://ctva.biz/US/TalkShow/TonightShow_1968-69_JohnnyCarson_s7.htm |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=The Classic TV Archive |archive-date=January 14, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250114122500/https://ctva.biz/US/TalkShow/TonightShow_1968-69_JohnnyCarson_s7.htm |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Bishop (Joey) papers (LSC.2260) |url=https://oac.cdlib.org/findaid/ark:/13030/c8pn9bb9/dsc/ |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=Online Archive of California}}</ref>}}

In [[1966 USAC Championship Car season|1966]], Andretti won his second straight USAC title. In contrast to his maiden title win, Andretti won eight of fifteen starts<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1966 USAC Champ Car Series Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1966/UO/ |access-date=2025-02-01 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=October 5, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231005005745/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1966/UO/ |url-status=live }}</ref> and led 1,142 laps, nearly 1,000 laps more than his closest competitor.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2015" /> He led 54.5% of all laps in 1966, a record until [[Al Unser]]'s 66.8% in [[1970 USAC Championship Car season|1970]], and still the second-highest figure in history as of the 2022 season.<ref name="Pryson–2022">{{Cite news |last=Pryson |first=Mike |date=2022-03-28 |title=Here's 10 IndyCar Records That Will Never Be Broken |url=https://www.autoweek.com/racing/indycar/g39564492/10-indycar-records-never-be-broken/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=[[Autoweek]] |language=en-US |archive-date=November 17, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251117083011/https://www.autoweek.com/racing/indycar/g39564492/10-indycar-records-never-be-broken/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti also took pole at the [[1966 Indianapolis 500]], but retired after 27 laps with a mechanical failure.<ref name="Indianapolis 500 Career">{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti Indianapolis 500 Career |url=https://www.indianapolismotorspeedway.com/events/indy500/history/historical-stats/driver-stats/drivers/mario-andretti |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=[[Indianapolis Motor Speedway]] |language=en |archive-date=August 1, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190801184109/https://www.indianapolismotorspeedway.com/events/indy500/history/historical-stats/driver-stats/drivers/mario-andretti |url-status=live }}</ref>

In [[1967 USAC Championship Car season|1967]], Andretti lost the season USAC championship to A. J. Foyt. Although Andretti won eight races, Foyt won the [[1967 Indianapolis 500]];<ref name="Andretti vs Foyt">{{Cite web |last=Kirby |first=Gordon |title=The Way It Is/ Andretti vs Foyt, Parnelli and the Unsers |url=https://gordonkirby.com/categories/columns/theway/2016/the_way_it_is_no531.html |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=Gordon Kirby |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194523/https://gordonkirby.com/categories/columns/theway/2016/the_way_it_is_no531.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti was on pole at Indianapolis but lost a wheel.<ref name="Andretti vs Foyt & Petty">{{Cite web |last=Kirby |first=Gordon |date=2014-12-12 |title=Andretti vs Foyt & Petty |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/us-scene/indycar/andretti-vs-foyt-petty/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194613/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/us-scene/indycar/andretti-vs-foyt-petty/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti fought through broken ribs to stay in the title race.<ref name="Andretti vs Foyt & Petty" /> Foyt carried a 340-point lead over Andretti going into the season-ending [[Budweiser 500K|Rex Mays 300]] at [[Riverside International Raceway|Riverside]].<ref name="Andretti vs Foyt & Petty" /> Andretti ran out of fuel with four laps to go and settled for third,<ref name="Andretti vs Foyt & Petty" /> costing him 180 points.<ref name="1967 Rex Mays 300">{{Cite web |title=1967 Rex Mays 300 |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/race-results/1967_Rex_Mays_300/UO/ |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=July 30, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220730091343/https://www.racing-reference.info/race-results/1967_Rex_Mays_300/UO/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Ordinarily, he would have won the championship anyway, as third place was worth 420 points and Foyt had crashed on lap 50.<ref name="1967 Rex Mays 300" /> However, Foyt's tire sponsor Goodyear arranged for him to commandeer [[Roger McCluskey]]'s car to prevent Andretti, a Firestone man, from winning.<ref name="Andretti vs Foyt & Petty" /><ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=|pages=179–180}} Foyt piloted McCluskey's car to fifth place. Despite a point deduction, he won the championship by 80 points.<ref name="MSM–American Comment">{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=American Comment |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/january-1969/23/american-comment/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 23, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250123081604/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/january-1969/23/american-comment/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti received his first [[Driver of the Year]] award but was deflated by how the season ended, saying, "I had the championship in my hands, and then it was gone."<ref name="Andretti vs Foyt & Petty" />

DVL owner Al Dean died at the end of the 1967 season. Per his wishes, the team was wound up. The estate sold the team's assets to Andretti, who became an owner-driver under the name Andretti Racing Enterprises.<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=|pages=181–182}} Brawner stayed on as chief mechanic.<ref>{{cite web |last=Zautke |first=Steve |date=January 10, 2014 |title=Andy Granatelli – They Called Him Mister 500 |url=http://racingnation.com/andy-granatelli-they-called-him-mister-500/ |access-date=July 30, 2021 |publisher=RacingNation |archive-date=August 3, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210803073632/https://racingnation.com/andy-granatelli-they-called-him-mister-500/ |url-status=live }}</ref> In [[1968 USAC Championship Car season|1968]], Andretti once again lost the title at the final race of the season at Riverside, but this time in a reversal of the events of 1967. Andretti held a 304-point lead over [[Bobby Unser]] at the start and led Unser on track by 47 seconds at one point. However, his engine failed on lap 58. He borrowed [[Joe Leonard]]'s car (whose brakes were dead<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=|pages=200}}) and then [[Lloyd Ruby]]'s car for the final stretch. He fought back to third, but received only 165 points instead of the usual 420 since only his laps in Ruby's car were counted.<ref>{{Cite web |title=1968 Rex Mays 300 |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/race-results/1968_Rex_Mays_300/UO/ |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=August 20, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220820002429/https://www.racing-reference.info/race-results/1968_Rex_Mays_300/UO/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Unser finished second, scoring 480 points.<ref name="MSM–American Comment" /> Unser won the title by 11 points, the narrowest margin in USAC history.<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=|pages=202}} Despite losing the title, Andretti set records for second-place finishes in a season (11 times in 27 starts) and podium finishes in a season (16), which still stand to this day.<ref name="Pryson–2022" />

|}[[File:AndrettiHawk.jpg|thumb|A replica of Andretti's [[1969 Indianapolis 500]]-winning [[Brawner Hawk]] at the [[Indianapolis Motor Speedway Museum]]]]Unhappy about being an owner-driver,<ref name="Miller–2020">{{Cite web |last=Miller |first=Robin |date=2020-08-22 |title=The 'Andretti Curse' |url=https://racer.com/2020/08/22/miller-the-andretti-curse/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=[[Racer (magazine)|RACER]] |language=en-US |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194552/https://racer.com/2020/08/22/miller-the-andretti-curse/ |url-status=live }}</ref> and concerned that Firestone was cutting back its sponsorship budget,<ref name="Andretti vs Foyt" /> Andretti sold the team to [[Andy Granatelli]]'s [[STP Corporation]] before the [[1969 USAC Championship Car season|1969]] season. Granatelli retained the DVL cars and staff, although Brawner disliked Granatelli and insisted that he not participate in racing decisions.<ref name="Kravitz–2021">{{Cite news |last=Kravitz |first=Bob |title='One more lap! One more lap!': An oral history of Mario Andretti's historic Indy 500 win 50 years ago |url=https://www.nytimes.com/athletic/984496/2019/05/23/one-more-lap-one-more-lap-an-oral-history-of-mario-andrettis-historic-indy-500-win-50-years-ago/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |work=The New York Times |date=May 13, 2021 |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331}}</ref> Andretti won nine races in 1969, including the [[1969 Indianapolis 500]] and the [[Pikes Peak International Hill Climb]].<ref name="ESPN" /> He won his third title and was named [[American Broadcasting Company|ABC]]'s [[Wide World of Sports (U.S. TV series)|Wide World of Sports]] Athlete of the Year.<ref>{{Citation |last=Vogan |first=Travis |chapter=Appendix 2: ABC's Wide World of Sports Athlete of the Year |date=2018-11-06 |title=ABC Sports: The Rise and Fall of Network Sports Television |pages=232–233 |url=https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1525/9780520966260-013/html?lang=en |access-date=2025-01-29 |publisher=University of California Press |language=en |doi=10.1525/9780520966260-013 |isbn=978-0-520-96626-0|url-access=subscription }}</ref> His 5,025 points were a USAC record, and he scored nearly twice as many points as runner-up [[Al Unser]] (2,630).<ref name="Andretti vs Foyt" /> His IndyCar prize money of $365,165 (including a $205,727 check for winning the Indianapolis 500) was, by one count, the largest single-season haul in the history of American sports to that point, and his total pay that year (including endorsements) was estimated at as much as $1 million.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Libby |first=Bill |url= |title=Andretti |publisher=Tempo Books |year=1971 |isbn=978-0-448-05429-2 |location=New York |pages=166, 172–173}}</ref>

==== 1970–1971: Team split and struggles ====
The core of the team split up after the 1969 title season, when Goodyear persuaded STP mechanics Clint Brawner and Jim McGee to start their own team.<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=206|pages=}} Andretti remained with STP, which agreed to sponsor him during the [[1970 Formula One season]] in a privateer [[March Engineering|March]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Radosta |first=John S. |date=1970-02-10 |title=Granatelli Plans Grand Prix Racing |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1970/02/10/archives/granatelli-plans-grand-prix-racing-to-use-3-formula-one-cars-with.html |access-date=2025-01-31 |work=The New York Times |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331}}</ref>

Various reasons were given for the split. Brawner said that he and McGee left because Granatelli and Firestone were underpaying them, and added that his old-school thinking clashed with Andretti and McGee's desire to innovate.<ref name="Indy 500 Mechanic" />{{rp||page=|pages=164–166}} He was particularly hurt that Andretti wanted to retire the old Brawner Hawk for a chassis from [[Team Lotus|Lotus]].<ref name="Indy 500 Mechanic" />{{rp||page=|pages=167–168}} However, it was also rumored that Andretti forced out Brawner, which Andretti denied.<ref name="Miller–2020" /> In his foreword to Brawner's 1975 autobiography, Andretti wrote that "we had our disagreements, but until things started turning sour near the end, we worked them out."<ref name="Indy 500 Mechanic" />{{rp||page=xii|pages=}} He added that "there are many reasons why our operation fell apart. ... Racing relationships are like Hollywood marriages: they seldom last long."<ref name="Indy 500 Mechanic" />{{rp||page=xiv|pages=}} McGee said that Andretti and Brawner had been "feuding for years,"<ref name="Glick–1994">{{Cite web |last=Glick |first=Shav |date=1994-04-09 |title=Mario's Finish Line : Phoenix Is Very Special in Andretti's Final Season and Crew Chief McGee Has Been a Big Part of His Career |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1994-04-09-sp-44042-story.html |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=Los Angeles Times |language=en-US |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194550/https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1994-04-09-sp-44042-story.html |url-status=live }}</ref> but "certainly respected each other."<ref name="Miller–2020" /> He opined that Brawner was unwilling to work for Granatelli.<ref name="Glick–1994" /> According to an [[urban legend]], Brawner's wife Kay hexed Andretti's family after the STP split, giving rise to the so-called "[[Andretti curse]]."<ref name="Miller–2020" />

Neither side fully recovered from the split. The Brawner/McGee team's financial backer went broke, and McGee returned to STP in 1971.<ref name="Indy 500 Mechanic" />{{rp||page=|pages=175–176}} Meanwhile, Andretti settled for a fifth-place finish in [[1970 USAC Championship Car season|1970]],<ref name="Racing–Reference">{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver/Mario_Andretti/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en |archive-date=January 29, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250129020555/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver/Mario_Andretti/ |url-status=live }}</ref> and the STP Formula One team shut down after one season.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Radosta |first=John S. |date=1970-08-02 |title=Andretti Weighs Several Offers To Race in Grand Prix Abroad |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1970/08/02/archives/andretti-weighs-several-offers-to-race-in-grand-prix-abroad.html |access-date=2025-01-31 |work=The New York Times |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=April 13, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250413083122/https://www.nytimes.com/1970/08/02/archives/andretti-weighs-several-offers-to-race-in-grand-prix-abroad.html |url-status=live }}</ref> In [[1971 USAC Championship Car season|1971]], Andretti fell to ninth in USAC's paved track championship.<ref name="Racing–Reference" /> He scored no points in the [[1971 USAC National Dirt Car Championship|dirt track standings]], with a best finish of 13th.<ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=45}}

=== Parnelli (1972–1975) ===
For the [[1971 USAC Championship Car season|1972]] season, Andretti left STP and joined [[Vel's Parnelli Jones Racing]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Radosta |first=John S. |date=1971-10-13 |title=About Motor Sports |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1971/10/13/archives/jones-race-team-gets-third-big-one-andretti.html |access-date=2025-01-31 |work=The New York Times |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194855/https://www.nytimes.com/1971/10/13/archives/jones-race-team-gets-third-big-one-andretti.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Parnelli was IndyCar's dominant team at the time, with 1970 champion [[Al Unser]] and 1971 champion [[Joe Leonard]].<ref name="Sports Illustrated–1978">{{Cite magazine |date=1978-10-02 |title=Bravo, Mario! |url=https://vault.si.com/vault/1978/10/02/bravo-mario-hard-charging-mario-andretti-experienced-both-triumph-and-tragedy-on-his-way-to-becoming-the-second-american-to-win-the-world-driving-championship |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220930000920/https://vault.si.com/vault/1978/10/02/bravo-mario-hard-charging-mario-andretti-experienced-both-triumph-and-tragedy-on-his-way-to-becoming-the-second-american-to-win-the-world-driving-championship |archive-date=September 30, 2022 |access-date=2025-01-31 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en-us |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti persuaded the team to hire Lotus designer [[Maurice Philippe]],<ref name="O'Keefe–2003">{{Cite web |last=O'Keefe |first=Thomas |date=2003-11-19 |title=Sixteen Sundays: Mario Andretti, Parnelli Jones & Team USA (part I) |url=https://atlasf1.autosport.com/2003/nov19/okeefe.html |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=Atlas F1 Magazine |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194647/https://atlasf1.autosport.com/2003/nov19/okeefe.html |url-status=live }}</ref> and Jim McGee also joined the team.<ref name="Glick–1994" /> The combination was expected to be a "superteam."<ref>{{Cite magazine |date=1972-08-07 |title='You Know Me, Al,' Said Joe |url=https://vault.si.com/vault/1972/08/07/you-know-me-al-said-joe |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210506061907/https://vault.si.com/vault/1972/08/07/you-know-me-al-said-joe |archive-date=May 6, 2021 |access-date=2025-01-31 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en-us |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Lerner–2021">{{Cite web |last=Lerner |first=Preston |date=2021-04-15 |title=The car that led Mario Andretti to his F1 title: Parnelli VPJ-4 |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/opinions/the-car-that-led-mario-andretti-to-his-f1-title-and-should-have-won-gps-parnelli-vpj-4/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=February 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250211222127/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/opinions/the-car-that-led-mario-andretti-to-his-f1-title-and-should-have-won-gps-parnelli-vpj-4/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=52}}

Andretti never won an IndyCar title with Parnelli.<ref name="Sports Illustrated–1978" /> In his three full-time IndyCar seasons with the team (1972–1974), Andretti finished 11th, 5th, and 14th,<ref name="Racing–Reference" /> while his teammate Leonard won the [[1972 USAC Championship Car season|1972]] title.<ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=48}} He did better on dirt tracks, winning the 1974 title after winning three out of five races.<ref name="SilverCrownStandings">{{Cite web |title=USAC Silver Crown Standings |url=https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/677a50b1faa4116f638e6593_USAC%20Silver%20Crown%20Standings.pdf |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=United States Auto Club |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194723/https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/677a50b1faa4116f638e6593_USAC%20Silver%20Crown%20Standings.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="SilverCrownWinners">{{Cite web |title=USAC Silver Crown Winners |url=https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/677a505aebec7ea5930836cc_USAC%20Silver%20Crown%20Winners%20List.pdf |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=United States Auto Club |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194650/https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/677a505aebec7ea5930836cc_USAC%20Silver%20Crown%20Winners%20List.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref> He nearly won the 1973 title as well, but teammate Al Unser beat him even though Andretti won two out of three races.<ref name="SilverCrownStandings" /><ref name="SilverCrownWinners" />{{efn|He skipped the 1972 season.<ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=49}}}}

During this period, Andretti was increasingly drawn to [[formula racing]]. He made guest appearances in [[Formula One]] with [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]] in [[1972 Formula One season|1972]],<ref name="Formula One–1972">{{Cite web |title=1972 Driver Standings: Mario Andretti |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1972/drivers/MARAND01/mario-andretti |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=February 12, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250212173429/https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1972/drivers/MARAND01/mario-andretti |url-status=live }}</ref> and raced in [[Formula 5000]] in [[1974 SCCA/USAC Formula 5000 Championship|1974]] and [[1975 SCCA/USAC Formula 5000 Championship|1975]].<ref name="Kirby–2013">{{Cite web |last=Kirby |first=Gordon |date=2013-12-02 |title=Brian Redman's American adventures |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/us-scene/brian-redmans-american-adventures/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194709/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/us-scene/brian-redmans-american-adventures/ |url-status=live }}</ref> In 1975, Andretti stopped competing full-time in IndyCar,<ref name="Mario Andretti Results">{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver/Mario_Andretti/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |work=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=January 29, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250129020555/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver/Mario_Andretti/ |url-status=live }}</ref> instead driving full-time for the Parnelli Formula One team.<ref name="Francis–2025">{{Cite web |last=Francis |first=Anna |date=2025-01-31 |title=7 times a driver signing transformed a team's fortunes |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/latest/article/from-an-underdog-win-to-record-breaking-success-7-times-a-driver-signing.47OvmSiAw7g07vkZiEUNsg |access-date=2025-02-02 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=February 3, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250203090518/https://www.formula1.com/en/latest/article/from-an-underdog-win-to-record-breaking-success-7-times-a-driver-signing.47OvmSiAw7g07vkZiEUNsg |url-status=live }}</ref> After quitting Formula One in early 1976,<ref name="O'Keefe–2003 part 2">{{Cite web |last=O'Keefe |first=Thomas |date=2003-11-26 |title=Sixteen Sundays: Mario Andretti, Parnelli Jones & Team USA (part II) |url=https://atlasf1.autosport.com/2003/nov26/okeefe.html |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=Atlas F1 Magazine |archive-date=April 11, 2025 === Penske (1976–1978) ===
While racing with [[Team Lotus]], Andretti appeared sporadically in IndyCar with McGee's new team, [[Penske Racing]].<ref name="Glick–1994" /> In nineteen races from 1976 to 1978, he won one race (at [[Trenton Speedway|Trenton]] in 1978) and collected eight top-five finishes.<ref name="Racing–Reference" /><ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=66}}

== Stock car racing career ==
At the height of his IndyCar career, Andretti also made thirty appearances in top-level [[stock car racing]] from 1965 to 1969. Along with [[A. J. Foyt]], he is one of two drivers to ever win [[NASCAR]]'s most prestigious race, the [[Daytona 500]], without being a full-time stock car driver.<ref name="Oreovicz–2017">{{cite web |last=Oreovicz |first=John |date=February 18, 2017 |title=Mario Andretti's Daytona 500 win 50 years ago one for the ages |url=https://www.espn.com/racing/nascar/story/_/id/18713973/nascar-incredible-career-mario-andretti-daytona-500-win-stands-out |access-date=February 20, 2017 |work=ESPN |publisher=ESPN Internet Ventures}}</ref>

In USAC, Andretti scored one win and eight top-five finishes in sixteen races from 1965 to 1968.<ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=|pages=21, 25, 27, 31}} His best season performance was 1967, when he competed in eight out of 22 races,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti |url=https://www.thethirdturn.com/wiki/Mario_Andretti |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=The Third Turn |language=en |archive-date=March 12, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250312180230/https://thethirdturn.com/wiki/Mario_Andretti |url-status=live }}</ref> won round 12 at [[Mosport]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=USAC National Stock Car Winners |url=https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/66e0b99a5838d3be4830f581_USAC%20Stock%20Car%20Winners.pdf |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=United States Auto Club |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194618/https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/66e0b99a5838d3be4830f581_USAC%20Stock%20Car%20Winners.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref> and finished seventh in the standings.<ref>{{Cite web |title=USAC National Stock Car Standings |url=https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/66e0b84a019f3165139d27c1_USAC%20Stock%20Car%20Points.pdf |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=United States Auto Club |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194544/https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/65ebb7989fc017a651da1582/66e0b84a019f3165139d27c1_USAC%20Stock%20Car%20Points.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref>

In the NASCAR [[NASCAR Cup Series|Grand National Series]], Andretti was less successful on average, with one win, one top-five finish, and three top tens in fourteen races from 1966 to 1969.<ref name="Mario Andretti Results" /> He primarily drove for Ford works team [[Holman-Moody]], securing the drive through his connections at Ford headquarters.<ref name="Sabotage">{{Cite news |last=Pruett |first=Marshall |date=2017-02-22 |title=Mario Andretti's Team Tried to Sabotage Him from Winning the Daytona 500 |url=https://www.roadandtrack.com/motorsports/a32710/mario-andretti-daytona-500-history/ |access-date=2025-01-31 |work=[[Road & Track]] |archive-date=October 17, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221017174455/https://www.roadandtrack.com/motorsports/a32710/mario-andretti-daytona-500-history/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He generally did not get the first pick of equipment and pit crews,<ref name="Jensen–2020">{{Cite web |last=Jensen |first=Tom |date=2020-11-20 |title=Lapping the Field at Daytona |url=https://www.nascarhall.com/blog/lapping-the-field-at-daytona |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Nascar Hall of Fame |language=en-US |archive-date=February 14, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250214184229/https://www.nascarhall.com/blog/lapping-the-field-at-daytona |url-status=live }}</ref> and said that a lack of technical support forced him to ask a rookie, [[Donnie Allison]], for help setting up his car.<ref name="1967Daytona500">{{Cite web |last=Smits |first=Garry |title=Mario Andretti honored 50 years after Daytona win |url=https://www.jacksonville.com/story/sports/2017/02/26/mario-andretti-honored-50-years-after-daytona-win/15744405007/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=The Florida Times-Union |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="Sabotage" /> ''[[Sports Illustrated]]'' noted that Andretti's setup favored [[oversteer]] (in American parlance, "loose"<ref>{{Cite web |title=Tight and Loose: Middle & High School |url=https://www.nascarhall.com/plan-a-visit/online-education/tight-and-loose-middle |access-date=2025-03-18 |website=Nascar Hall of Fame |language=en-US |archive-date=March 18, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250318190015/https://www.nascarhall.com/plan-a-visit/online-education/tight-and-loose-middle |url-status=live }}</ref>) to an extent that was considered extreme at the time.<ref name="Sports Illustrated–1978" /> After convincing the team to give him a top-spec engine,<ref name="Jensen–2020" /> he won the [[1967 Daytona 500]], but alleged that the team tried to sabotage his race so that its lead driver, [[Fred Lorenzen]], could inherit the win.<ref name="Jensen–2020" /><ref name="1967Daytona500" /> His friend [[Parnelli Jones]] backed up the accusation.<ref name="Oreovicz–2017" /> Andretti stopped competing in NASCAR after 1969, as race seats at teams of the caliber of Holman-Moody rarely came open after the 1960s.<ref>{{Cite AV media |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kXT7BF2Ouso |title=Mario Andretti: Addicted To Adrenaline |date=2024-06-12 |last=Dale Earnhardt Jr.'s Dirty Mo Media |access-date=2025-01-28 |via=YouTube |archive-date=December 19, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241219230735/https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kXT7BF2Ouso |url-status=live }} (53:05-54:27)</ref>

In the 1970s and 1980s, Andretti competed in six editions of the [[International Race of Champions]] (IROC), an invitational stock car series with a limited calendar. He won [[IROC VI]] and finished second in [[IROC III]] and [[IROC V]]. He won three races in twenty events.<ref name="Mario Andretti Results" />

== Formula One career ==
=== Part-time roles (1968–1970) ===
[[File:AndrettiMario19690801Lotus63-Allrad-3.jpg|thumb|Andretti driving the [[Lotus 63]] at the [[1969 German Grand Prix]].]]
Although the [[Indianapolis 500]] dropped off the Formula One calendar in 1960, some teams continued racing at Indianapolis, including [[Colin Chapman]]'s [[Team Lotus]].<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Jeffries |first1=Tom |last2=Malsher-Lopez |first2=David |date=2022-05-25 |title=Formula One drivers who conquered the Indy 500 |url=https://www.motorsport.com/indycar/news/f1-drivers-who-have-won-the-indy-500/6516016/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250114180615/https://www.motorsport.com/indycar/news/f1-drivers-who-have-won-the-indy-500/6516016/ |archive-date=January 14, 2025 |access-date=2025-02-01 |work=[[Motorsport.com]] |publisher=[[Motorsport Network]] |language=en |url-status=live }}</ref> At the [[1965 Indianapolis 500]], Lotus star [[Jim Clark]] won and Andretti finished third as the top-placed rookie.<ref name="FormulaOneBio">{{cite web |author=Gerald Donaldson|authorlink=Gerald Donaldson |title=Mario Andretti |url=http://www.formula1.com/teams_and_drivers/hall_of_fame/242/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070629190346/http://www.formula1.com/teams_and_drivers/hall_of_fame/242/ |archive-date=June 29, 2007 |access-date=July 16, 2007 |website=Formula One}}</ref><ref name="Taylor–2007">{{Cite journal |last=Taylor |first=Simon |date=March 2007 |title=Lunch with ... Mario Andretti |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/march-2007/32/lunch-mario-andretti/ |journal=Motor Sport |volume=LXXXIII |page=36 |archive-date=May 24, 2021 |access-date=January 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210524220813/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/march-2007/32/lunch-mario-andretti/ |url-status=live }}</ref> On Clark's recommendation,<ref name="Hughes–2020">{{Cite web |last=Hughes |first=Mark |date=2020-05-04 |title=MPH Grand Prix debuts: the sensational arrival of Mario Andretti |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/mark-hughes/mph-grand-prix-debuts-the-sensational-arrival-of-mario-andretti/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=February 13, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250213014212/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/mark-hughes/mph-grand-prix-debuts-the-sensational-arrival-of-mario-andretti/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Chapman invited Andretti to race in Formula One, saying, "When you're ready, call me."<ref name="Taylor–2007" /><ref name="Vaughn–2019">{{Cite web |last=Vaughn |first=Mark |date=2019-04-17 |title=Mario Andretti on A.J., aging and the Andretti luck |url=https://www.autoweek.com/car-life/a2133596/mario-andretti-dishes-aj-aging-and-andretti-luck/ |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=[[Autoweek]] |language=en-US |archive-date=January 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250126204827/https://www.autoweek.com/car-life/a2133596/mario-andretti-dishes-aj-aging-and-andretti-luck/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

Andretti joined Lotus for the [[1968 Italian Grand Prix]].<ref name="MSM–2016">{{Cite web |date=2016-08-17 |title=1968 Italian Grand Prix race report: Kiwis' sweet victory |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/october-1968/15/the-39th-italian-grand-prix/ |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250126231957/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/october-1968/15/the-39th-italian-grand-prix/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He was delighted by the [[Lotus 49|Lotus 49B]], saying that its handling was a major improvement over IndyCar.<ref name="Hughes–2020" /> He beat the [[Monza Circuit|Monza]] lap record in testing,<ref name="MSM–2016" /> but was disqualified after flying back to America for a contractually required race.<ref name="MSM–2016" /> He later said that the Monza officials broke a promise to waive the applicable rule on his behalf.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Baldwin |first=Alan |date=2014-09-05 |title=F1 legend Andretti relives Monza memories |url=https://www.reuters.com/article/sports/f1-legend-andretti-relives-monza-memories-idUSKBN0H014B/ |access-date=2025-01-26 |work=Reuters |archive-date=April 12, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250412181954/https://www.reuters.com/article/sports/f1-legend-andretti-relives-monza-memories-idUSKBN0H014B/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Vaughn–2019" />

Andretti got his real start in Formula One at the [[1968 United States Grand Prix]] and took pole.<ref name="FormulaOneBio" /><ref>{{Cite journal  | last = Taylor  | first = Simon  | title = Lunch with ... Mario Andretti  | journal = Motorsport  | volume = LXXXIII | pages = 33–42  |date=March 2007 }}</ref> Due to his disqualification at Monza (where he had qualified tenth),<ref>{{Cite web |title=Italy 1968 - Qualifications |url=https://www.statsf1.com/en/1968/italie/qualification.aspx |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=STATS F1 |archive-date=November 15, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241115192829/https://www.statsf1.com/en/1968/italie/qualification.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref> he became the first Formula One driver to start his first race from pole.<ref name="Hughes–2020" /> [[Jackie Stewart]] overtook him on the first lap, but the two drivers were neck-and-neck until Andretti's nose cone broke, forcing him to pit. He eventually retired with a clutch failure, but he had made a strong impression. Reviewing the race'', [[Motor Sport (magazine)|Motor Sport]]'' wrote that Andretti displayed "that same assurance of absolute control [in the corners] one saw in [Jim] Clark's driving."<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1968 United States Grand Prix race report: Stewart dictates the pace |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/november-1968/20/10th-united-states-grands-prix/ |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250126231958/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/november-1968/20/10th-united-states-grands-prix/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

{{Quote box
| quote = It's my neck. I put it under the guillotine every time I climb into a race car. So if somebody puts a price on it, I am going to study the sales tag—very carefully.
| author = Mario Andretti
| source = ''What's it Like Out There?''<ref name="Bantam–1970" />{{rp||page=151}}
| align = left
| width = 300px
}}

At the end of the 1968 season, Chapman offered Andretti a full-time drive to replace Clark, who had died in [[1968 Deutschland Trophäe|an accident]] that April. Andretti declined, not wishing to give up his stable USAC career. For the next two years, he made only sporadic appearances in Formula One with Lotus and [[STP (motor oil company)|STP]]-[[March Engineering|March]].<ref name="FormulaOneBio" /> The cars were mostly uncompetitive, and he finished only one race in his first three seasons.<ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=42}} At the one race he finished, the [[1970 Spanish Grand Prix]], he collected his first Formula One podium after several drivers ahead of him retired with mechanical issues.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1970 Spanish Grand Prix race report: Stewart the matador |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/may-1970/26/the-grand-prix-of-spain-ole/ |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250126231957/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/may-1970/26/the-grand-prix-of-spain-ole/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Ferrari (1971–1972) ===
Andretti signed with [[Scuderia Ferrari]] in {{F1|1971}} and entered seven out of 11 races, completing two. In his Ferrari debut, he achieved his maiden Grand Prix win at [[1971 South African Grand Prix|Kyalami]] after race leader [[Denny Hulme]]'s engine failed with four laps to go.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1971 South African Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/april-1971/44/fifth-south-african-grand-prix/ |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250126231958/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/april-1971/44/fifth-south-african-grand-prix/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He also won the non-championship [[1971 Questor Grand Prix|Questor Grand Prix]] in California. Following the Questor win, [[Enzo Ferrari]] offered to make Andretti his No. 1 driver for 1972, but Andretti declined, later remarking that "[Formula One] didn't pay much back then [...] but I always figured I'd get another opportunity."<ref name="Ferrari1971">{{cite web |last=Medland |first=Chris |date=2021-03-07 |title=Retro: Mario Andretti on his first F1 win with Ferrari, 50 years ago |url=https://racer.com/2021/03/07/retro-mario-andretti-on-his-first-f1-win-with-ferrari-50-years-ago/ |access-date=2022-08-02 |work=[[Racer (magazine)|RACER]] |archive-date=December 5, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241205104958/https://racer.com/2021/03/07/retro-mario-andretti-on-his-first-f1-win-with-ferrari-50-years-ago/ |url-status=dead }}</ref> Andretti also raced five times in {{F1|1972}}, but scored no podiums.<ref name="Formula One–1972" /> He did not compete in the {{F1|1973}} season.<ref>{{Cite web |title=1973 Driver Standings |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1973/drivers |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=January 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250126064502/https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1973/drivers |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Parnelli (1974–1976) ===
In the mid-1970s, Andretti encouraged [[Vel's Parnelli Jones Racing|Parnelli]], his IndyCar team, to sponsor a Formula One car.<ref name="Blackstock–2024">{{Cite web |last=Blackstock |first=Elizabeth |date=2024-08-12 |title=Meet the man who gave Mario Andretti his first full-time F1 ride |url=https://www.planetf1.com/features/parnelli-jones-gave-mario-andretti-full-time-f1-ride |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=PlanetF1 |language=en |archive-date=December 19, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241219204230/https://www.planetf1.com/features/parnelli-jones-gave-mario-andretti-full-time-f1-ride |url-status=live }}</ref> To prepare for a Formula One challenge, the team secured funding from [[Firestone Tire and Rubber Company|Firestone]],<ref name="Blackstock–2024" /><ref name="Vaughn–2019" /> which agreed to make special tires for the team.<ref name="Lerner–2021" /> In addition to Maurice Philippe, the team hired more Lotus veterans, including Jim Clark's old crew chief Dick Scammell and administrator Andrew Ferguson.<ref name="O'Keefe–2003" />

Parnelli ran Andretti in the two North American end-of-season races in {{F1|1974}}.<ref name="Vaughn–2019" /> He qualified third at the {{F1 GP|1974|United States}} but did not start the race due to a mechanical failure.<ref name="O'Keefe–2003" /><ref name="Lerner–2021" /> Parnelli also ran Andretti in the North American [[Formula 5000]] series in [[1974 SCCA/USAC Formula 5000 Championship|1974]] and [[1975 SCCA/USAC Formula 5000 Championship|1975]], both times finishing second to [[Brian Redman]].<ref name="Kirby–2013" /> In each season, Andretti won as many races as Redman, but his results were less consistent.<ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=|pages=52–56}}

In [[1975 Formula One season|1975]], Andretti became a full-time Formula One driver for the first time.<ref name="Francis–2025" /> He was disappointed by the [[Parnelli VPJ4]], which he felt was derivative of the [[Lotus 72]]. More importantly, sponsor Firestone pulled out ahead of the season.<ref name="O'Keefe–2003" /> The VPJ4 had been designed for Firestone's custom tires, and without them, its performance suffered.<ref name="Lerner–2021" /> The car also suffered from frequent brake failures.<ref name="O'Keefe–2003 part 2" /> At the {{F1 GP|1975|Spanish}}, Andretti qualified fourth and reached first after a multi-car crash on the first lap. However, the crash damaged his suspension, forcing his eventual retirement.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1975 Spanish Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/june-1975/36/the-spanish-grand-prix-catastrophic/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=February 17, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250217231100/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/june-1975/36/the-spanish-grand-prix-catastrophic/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He finished third at the non-championship [[1975 BRDC International Trophy]] Race.<ref name="O'Keefe–2003" /> At the {{F1 GP|1975|Swedish}}, he was nearly killed when his brakes failed during qualifying, but finished fourth with the team's backup car.<ref name="Lerner–2021" /> He finished 14th in the Drivers' Championship, scoring five points.<ref>{{Cite web |title=1975 Driver Standings |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1975/drivers |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=February 14, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250214113328/https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1975/drivers |url-status=live }}</ref>

Parnelli skipped the first race of the [[1976 Formula One season|1976 season]],<ref name="O'Keefe–2003 part 2" /> so Andretti started the year with Lotus and returned to Parnelli for the next two races.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1976 Brazilian Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/march-1976/42/brazilian-grand-prix-12/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 19, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250119150049/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/march-1976/42/brazilian-grand-prix-12/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Vaughn–2019" /> Parnelli pulled out of Formula One after round three when sponsor [[Viceroy (cigarette)|Viceroy]] withdrew funding.<ref name="8W" /> Andretti only learned of the decision when a reporter asked him about it as the grid lined up to start the race.<ref name="O'Keefe–2003 part 2" /><ref name="Lerner–2021" /> He later admitted that "I was the only one, really, that wanted [the Formula One team]."<ref name="Vaughn–2019" />

=== Lotus (1976–1980) ===
[[File:Chapman and Andretti at 1978 Dutch Grand Prix.jpg|thumb|Andretti and [[Colin Chapman]] at the [[1978 Dutch Grand Prix]]|left]]

==== 1976 ====
