The day after Andretti learned Parnelli was shutting down, he met Lotus' Colin Chapman, who told him, "I wish I had a decent car for you."<ref name="Vaughn–2019" /><ref name="O'Keefe–2003 part 2" /> Andretti took the Lotus job anyway, promising Chapman that "we will make the car better."<ref name="Lerner–2021" /> He negotiated for number one driver status, mindful of Chapman's reputation for giving only one driver the best machinery.<ref name="Vaughn–2019" /> With this authority, he borrowed his teammate's car when it was faster at a particular circuit,<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1977 Japanese Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/december-1977/44/japanese-grand-prix/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194731/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/december-1977/44/japanese-grand-prix/ |url-status=live }}</ref> or when his own car was unavailable.<ref name="Hamlyn–1979">{{Cite book |last1=Roebuck |first1=Nigel |url= |title=Mario Andretti: World Champion |last2=Andretti |first2=Mario |publisher=Hamlyn |year=1979 |isbn=978-0-600-39469-3 |location=London}}</ref>{{rp||page=59|pages=}}

The [[Lotus 77]] was not competitive, and with five races to go, Andretti had scored just five points, leaving him mired in 13th place.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Austria 1976 - Championship |url=https://www.statsf1.com/en/1976/autriche/championnat.aspx |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=STATS F1 |archive-date=May 7, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210507222730/https://www.statsf1.com/en/1976/autriche/championnat.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref> He asked to switch to the next year's car in mid-season, but Chapman declined.<ref name="Hamlyn–1979" />{{rp||page=|pages=56–57}} At the {{F1 GP|1976|Dutch}}, Andretti scored his first podium since March 1971. He collected three podiums in the final five races and lapped the field in his victory at the season-ending {{F1 GP|1976|Japanese}}.<ref name="FormulaOneBio" /> The late-season flurry of results moved Andretti up to 6th in the Drivers' Championship, with 22 points.<ref>{{Cite web |title=1976 Driver Standings |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1976/drivers |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=February 9, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250209150644/https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1976/drivers |url-status=live }}</ref>

===== Ground effect revolution =====
{{Multiple image
| perrow      = 2
| total_width = 350
| image1      = Lotus 77 Mont-Tremblant 04.JPG
| alt1        = The Lotus 77
| image2      = Lotus 78 front National Motor Museum, Beaulieu.jpg
| alt2        = The Lotus 78
| footer      = After replacing 1976's unsuccessful [[Lotus 77]] '''(first image)''', 1977's [[Lotus 78]] became one of the most influential cars in Formula One history. Its [[Ground effect (cars)|ground effect]] design dominated the sport until Formula One banned it in 1982,<ref name="GroundEffect2022" /> after which Andretti won a [[Championship Auto Racing Teams|CART]] title with the ground-effect [[Lola T800]].<ref name="Newman/Haas's first championship">{{Cite web |last=Kirby |first=Gordon |title=The Way It Is/ Newman/Haas's first championship |url=https://www.gordonkirby.com/categories/columns/theway/2017/the_way_it_is_no578.html |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Gordon Kirby |archive-date=September 17, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240917225334/http://gordonkirby.com/categories/columns/theway/2017/the_way_it_is_no578.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Ground effect cars returned to Formula One in 2022.<ref name="van Osten–2022">{{Cite web |last=van Osten |first=Phillip |date=2022-02-06 |title=Andretti puzzled by F1's move towards 'wider cars and bigger wings' |url=https://f1i.com/news/431388-andretti-puzzled-by-f1s-move-towards-wider-cars-and-bigger-wings.html |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=F1i.com |language=en |archive-date=January 29, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250129200937/https://f1i.com/news/431388-andretti-puzzled-by-f1s-move-towards-wider-cars-and-bigger-wings.html |url-status=live }}</ref> | align = right
}}
Andretti's timing was fortuitous, as he rejoined Lotus at the eve of the [[Ground effect (cars)|ground effect]] revolution. Since mid-1975, Lotus had been trying to shape the car to generate [[downforce]] (making the car faster in the corners) without a large rear wing (whose [[drag (physics)|drag]] would make the car slower on the straights). The Lotus design team added sidepods with vents to take in air, which was then channeled under the floor to facilitate the [[Venturi effect]]. The car was effectively sucked towards the ground, allowing it to take corners at unusually high speeds.<ref name="Formula One–2022">{{Cite web |last1=Hughes |first1=Mark |last2=Piola |first2=Giorgio |date=2022-02-15 |title=Tech Tuesday: The Lotus 79, F1's ground effect marvel |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/latest/article/tech-tuesday-the-lotus-79-f1s-ground-effect-marvel.sAD9PXt7mC8iMSwwe6CCw |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=January 29, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250129012504/https://www.formula1.com/en/latest/article/tech-tuesday-the-lotus-79-f1s-ground-effect-marvel.sAD9PXt7mC8iMSwwe6CCw |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti, whose STP-March team had experimented with sidepods in 1970,<ref name="van Osten–2022" /> encouraged the team to make the sidepods even bigger.<ref name="Pruett–2018">{{Cite web |last=Pruett |first=Marshall |date=2018-12-31 |title=The Last F1 Car to Carry an American to Victory |url=https://www.roadandtrack.com/motorsports/a25712728/lotus-79-mario-andretti-1978/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=[[Road & Track]] |language=en-US |archive-date=January 27, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250127001626/https://www.roadandtrack.com/motorsports/a25712728/lotus-79-mario-andretti-1978/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

Andretti, who received praise on several occasions for his technical feedback,<ref name="Indy 500 Mechanic" />{{rp||page=138|pages=}}<ref name="Sports Illustrated–1978" /> took a close interest in developing the car.<ref name="Pruett–2018" /> He knew that Lotus had a reputation for dangerous designs and worked with his mechanics to ensure that Chapman did not do anything "too radical."<ref name="Gushue–2016" /> [[Wind tunnel]] technology was still primitive at the time, but Lotus devised a way to model air flow on track by hiring a photographer to take pictures of wind-sensitive bristles that were mounted on the chassis in tests.<ref name="Pruett–2018" /> While testing the car at [[Hockenheimring|Hockenheim]], Andretti noticed that the car's downforce was much stronger when he drove close to a nearby fence. Chapman added sideskirts to keep the air flowing in one direction.<ref name="Pruett–2018" /><ref>"Mario Andretti: Addicted to Adrenaline" (2024), at 1:08-1:10.</ref>

Andretti also helped the team with his ability to set up a car; one commentator said that "aside from Andretti, only [[Niki Lauda|Lauda]] was known for great technical understanding [...] an increasingly vital quality for racecar drivers as racecars became increasingly sophisticated."<ref>{{Cite web |last=Malsher |first=David |date=2013-09-09 |title=Retro: Mario Andretti, World Champion - part 1, the foundations of glory |url=https://racer.com/2013/09/09/retro-mario-andretti-world-champion-part-1-the-foundations-of-glory/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=[[Racer (magazine)|RACER]] |language=en-US |archive-date=August 19, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250819222151/https://racer.com/2013/09/09/retro-mario-andretti-world-champion-part-1-the-foundations-of-glory |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti said that "if people say I'm overly obsessed with setting up my car, that's up to them ... I make tiny adjustments to the car, and I can feel them."<ref name="Hamlyn–1979" />{{rp||page=168|pages=}} Drawing on his extensive USAC oval racing experience, Andretti optimized his cars for each track by exploiting subtle differences in tire size ('stagger') and suspension set-up ('cross weighting') on each side of the car.<ref>{{cite book | last = Nye | first = Doug | title = Autocourse History of the Grand Prix car 1966 – 1985 | publisher = Hazleton Publishing | year = 1986 | isbn = 0-905138-37-6 |pages=95–97 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Nye |first=Doug |date=2020-01-13 |title=Lotus 78: F1's first true ground effect car |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/february-2020/111/lotus-78-2/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=February 3, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250203173846/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/february-2020/111/lotus-78-2/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Engineer Nigel Bennett recalled that Andretti would request seemingly imperceptible adjustments before the race, such as "Lower the front springs by an eighth of a turn."<ref name="Fearnley–2018">{{Cite web |last=Fearnley |first=Paul |date=2018-05-24 |title=Black gold: the Lotus 79's debut |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/single-seaters/f1/black-gold-lotus-79s-debut/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 27, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250127001626/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/single-seaters/f1/black-gold-lotus-79s-debut/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

==== 1977: Reliability issues ====
[[File:Monza 1977 - Mario Andretti - Vainqueur et Recordman du tour.jpg|thumb|Andretti won his first Italian Grand Prix in [[1977 Italian Grand Prix|1977]], piloting the revolutionary [[Lotus 78]].|left]]
In {{F1|1977}}, the [[Lotus 78]] was one of the fastest cars on the grid, and Andretti won four races, more than any other driver. At [[1977 Belgian Grand Prix|Zolder]], Andretti took pole by 1.54 seconds, infuriating Chapman, who wanted to hide the car's quality from his competitors.<ref name="Formula One–2022" />

At round four, Andretti won the [[1977 United States Grand Prix West|United States Grand Prix West]].<ref>{{cite web |title=United States Grand Prix history |url=http://www.formula1.com/news/features/2004/6/1739.html |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930211803/http://www.formula1.com/news/features/2004/6/1739.html |archive-date=September 30, 2007 |access-date=July 12, 2007 |publisher=[[Formula One]]}}</ref> He scored a dominant win at the {{F1 GP|1977|Spanish}},<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1977 Spanish Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/june-1977/24/the-spanish-grand-prix-8/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194542/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/june-1977/24/the-spanish-grand-prix-8/ |url-status=live }}</ref> but also held his own under close racing, winning the {{F1 GP|1977|French}} after a dramatic last-lap pass on [[John Watson (racing driver)|John Watson]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1977 French Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/august-1977/50/the-grand-prix-of-france-2/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194610/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/august-1977/50/the-grand-prix-of-france-2/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He also won his first {{F1 GP|1977|Italian}} after three attempts, an achievement in which he took great pride.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1977 Italian Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/october-1977/23/italian-grand-prix-7/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=June 24, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240624174727/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/october-1977/23/italian-grand-prix-7/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="Andretti–2016" /> Andretti concluded that the Lotus 78 was his favorite Formula One car, even more than the next year's title-winning Lotus 79.<ref>{{Cite web |last= |date=2015-10-12 |title=Great racing cars: 1977 Lotus 78 |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/single-seaters/f1/great-racing-cars-1977-lotus-78/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=November 23, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231123063004/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/single-seaters/f1/great-racing-cars-1977-lotus-78/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

Other than the wins, Andretti endured a snakebit season. Lotus had commissioned special engines, which proved to be unreliable,<ref name="Formula One–2022" /> and Andretti suffered engine failures while leading at [[1977 Austrian Grand Prix|Spielberg]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=2015-04-01 |title=1977 Austrian Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/september-1977/25/austrian-grand-prix-7/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194548/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/september-1977/25/austrian-grand-prix-7/ |url-status=live }}</ref> in second at [[1977 British Grand Prix|Silverstone]],<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1977 British Grand Prix race report - A marathon affair |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/august-1977/23/the-british-grand-prix-10/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 22, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250122004752/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/august-1977/23/the-british-grand-prix-10/ |url-status=live }}</ref> and battling for third at [[1977 Dutch Grand Prix|Zandvoort]].<ref name="Hamlyn–1979" />{{rp||page=|pages=66–67}} His engine also failed at [[1977 German Grand Prix|Hockenheim]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2015-04-02 |title=1977 German Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/september-1977/54/hockenheim-formula-1-race/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=February 12, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250212080834/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/september-1977/54/hockenheim-formula-1-race/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Lotus' [[Peter Wright (engineer)|Peter Wright]] and [[Ralph Bellamy (racing car designer)|Ralph Bellamy]] felt that if Chapman had settled for a regular [[Cosworth DFV]] engine, Lotus would have won the title.<ref name="Fearnley–2018" /><ref>{{Cite web |last=Cooper |first=Adam |date=2016-07-19 |title=Lotus 78: the car that started F1's ground-effect revolution |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/august-2007/72/ground-force/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 23, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240423073522/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/august-2007/72/ground-force/ |url-status=live }}</ref> For his own part, Andretti rued Chapman's tendency to "pull the last litre or two of fuel out of the cars before the race," noting that he ran out of fuel at three races in 1977 ([[1977 South African Grand Prix|Kyalami]], [[1977 Swedish Grand Prix|Anderstorp]], and [[1977 Canadian Grand Prix|Mosport]]).<ref>{{Cite web |last= |date=2015-10-12 |title=Great racing cars: 1977 Lotus 78 |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/single-seaters/f1/great-racing-cars-1977-lotus-78/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=November 23, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231123063004/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/single-seaters/f1/great-racing-cars-1977-lotus-78/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti also retired in third at [[1977 Brazilian Grand Prix|Interlagos]] with an electrical failure,<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1977 Brazilian Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/march-1977/55/brazilian-grand-prix-9/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 30, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250130054832/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/march-1977/55/brazilian-grand-prix-9/ |url-status=live }}</ref> and crashed at Zolder while fighting for the lead, which he called "one of the biggest mistakes of [his] career."<ref name="Andretti–2016" /> Ferrari dominated the Constructors' Championship with 95 (97){{Efn|At the time, Formula One's scoring system for the Constructors' Championship deducted the worst results from the first nine races of the season and the last eight races of the season. As such, the two points Ferrari scored at the [[1977 French Grand Prix]] did not count towards the season standings.}} points to Lotus' 62,<ref>{{Cite web |title=1977 Constructor Standings |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1977/team |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=January 25, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250125015452/https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1977/team |url-status=live }}</ref> and Andretti finished third in the Drivers' Championship, with 47 points, 25 behind Ferrari's [[Niki Lauda]],<ref>{{Cite web |title=1977 Driver Standings |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1977/drivers |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=December 31, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241231123640/https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1977/drivers |url-status=live }}</ref> who skipped the last two races.<ref name="Hamlyn–1979" />{{rp||page=73|pages=}}

==== 1978: World Champion ====
[[File:Mario Andretti 1978 World Championship Winning Lotus 79 (49379922591).jpg|thumb|Andretti's title-winning [[Lotus 79]], dubbed the "Black Beauty."<ref>{{Cite news |date=2023-02-02 |title=Mario Andretti's F1 title-winning Lotus Type 79 could fetch nearly $10m at auction |url=https://www.topgear.com/car-news/formula-one/mario-andrettis-f1-title-winning-lotus-type-79-could-fetch-nearly-10m-auction |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240917194607/https://www.topgear.com/car-news/formula-one/mario-andrettis-f1-title-winning-lotus-type-79-could-fetch-nearly-10m-auction |archive-date=2024-09-17 |access-date=2025-01-28 |work=Top Gear |language=en}}</ref>]]
Andretti won his first and only Formula One World Drivers' Championship in {{F1|1978}}. Before the season, the team signed [[Ronnie Peterson]] and made him the highest-paid driver in Formula One.<ref name="Ferrari1971" /> Although Chapman agreed to pay Andretti the same salary,<ref name="Ferrari1971" /> Andretti felt that he had earned number one driver status given how much time he had invested to develop the car.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Smith |first=Damien |date=2020-03-31 |title=Mario Andretti: "The Old Man offered me twice the money I was getting from Colin" |url=https://www.goodwood.com/grr/f1/mario-andretti-the-old-man-offered-me-twice-the-money-i-was-getting-from-colin/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=Goodwood Road & Racing |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194751/https://www.goodwood.com/grr/f1/mario-andretti-the-old-man-offered-me-twice-the-money-i-was-getting-from-colin/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Enzo Ferrari offered to double Andretti's salary, but withdrew the offer after Chapman "raised hell with [Enzo]".<ref name="Ferrari1971" /> Chapman placated Andretti by offering him a bonus of $10,000 a point.<ref name="Ferrari1971" /> In addition, Chapman promised to impose [[team orders]] to give Andretti the lead if Lotus was leading 1–2.<ref name="Hamlyn–1979" />{{rp||page=|pages=77–78}}

The team stayed with the 78 for the first five races while Chapman perfected the next car. At the season-opening {{F1 GP|1978|Argentine}}, Andretti took pole and led from start to finish.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1978 Argentine Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/february-1978/67/the-argentine-grand-prix/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=March 27, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250327160435/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/february-1978/67/the-argentine-grand-prix/ |url-status=live }}</ref> After five races, he was tied for second place in the standings with 18 points, five adrift of [[Patrick Depailler]].<ref>{{Cite news |last=Daley |first=Robert |date=1978-05-15 |title=Mario Andretti And a Dream |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1978/05/15/archives/mario-andretti-and-a-dream.html |access-date=2025-01-31 |work=The New York Times |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=August 30, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240830190731/https://www.nytimes.com/1978/05/15/archives/mario-andretti-and-a-dream.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

Lotus unveiled the [[Lotus 79]] at the {{F1 GP|1978|Belgian}}. The new car included an improved diffuser to facilitate airflow at the back of the car.<ref name="Pruett–2018" /> With plenty of downforce in hand, Lotus ran a small rear wing that increased the car's top speed,<ref>{{Cite web |last=Elson |first=James |date=2022-10-13 |title=Mario Andretti's greatest racing cars – Indy, F1 and Le Mans |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/single-seaters/f1/mario-andrettis-greatest-racing-cars/ |access-date=2025-01-26 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250126235643/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/single-seaters/f1/mario-andrettis-greatest-racing-cars/ |url-status=live }}</ref> fixing what Andretti felt was the 78's biggest weakness.<ref name="van Osten–2022" /> The 79 did introduce a new weakness, as a design flaw overheated the brake fluid.<ref name="Pruett–2018" /> Andretti's smooth driving style suited the car, whose downforce was so great that the chassis might have buckled in the hands of a more choppy driver.<ref name="Pruett–2018" /> At Belgium, Andretti took pole by eight-tenths of a second, led from start to finish, and won by ten seconds.<ref name="Fearnley–2018" />

Andretti dominated the rest of the season, winning five of the next eight races, while teammate Peterson finished second with two wins.<ref name="ddavid">{{cite web |title=Lotus – Ford 79 |url=http://www.ddavid.com/formula1/lotus79.htm |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110605093433/http://www.ddavid.com/formula1/lotus79.htm |archive-date=June 5, 2011 |access-date=July 13, 2007}}</ref> Lotus had four 1–2 finishes in 1978, and Andretti won them all, generating speculation that Chapman had ordered Peterson to let Andretti win.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Tytler |first=Ewan M. |date=1998 |title=Do Champions Need Team Orders? |url=https://atlasf1.autosport.com/98/hun/preview/tytler.html |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=Atlas F1 Magazine |archive-date=January 28, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250128155458/https://atlasf1.autosport.com/98/hun/preview/tytler.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Two rounds before Andretti clinched the title, Peterson denied being ordered to let Andretti by at any point,<ref>{{Cite web |last=Malsher |first=David |date=2013-09-10 |title=Retro: Mario Andretti, World Champion - part 2, winning the title |url=https://racer.com/2013/09/10/retro-mario-andretti-world-champion-part-2-winning-the-title/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=[[Racer (magazine)|RACER]] |language=en-US |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194527/https://racer.com/2013/09/10/retro-mario-andretti-world-champion-part-2-winning-the-title/ |url-status=live }}</ref> which Andretti repeated after the season.<ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=66}} However, Peterson then "ostentatiously" followed Andretti to a 1–2 finish at [[1978 Dutch Grand Prix|Zandvoort]].<ref name="8W" />

Andretti clinched the championship at the [[1978 Italian Grand Prix|Italian Grand Prix]], with two races to go.<ref name="sportscentury" /> He did not celebrate, as Peterson had suffered a major crash and died later that night due to complications from leg surgery.<ref name="sportscentury" /><ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=66}} Outside the hospital, Andretti laconically said, "Unhappily, motor racing is also this."<ref name="Roebuck–2000">{{Cite web |last=Roebuck |first=Nigel |date=2000-09-20 |title="I was put on this earth to drive race cars" |url=https://www.espn.com/auto/formulaone/news/2000/0920/760531.html |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=ESPN |archive-date=March 4, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250304220528/https://www.espn.com/auto/formulaone/news/2000/0920/760531.html |url-status=live }}</ref> In 2018, Andretti said that "I could never truly celebrate and I never will. It was an enormous jolt. You never really totally recover from [it]."<ref name="Pruett–2018" />

==== 1979–1980 ====
Andretti never won another Grand Prix after 1978. Following the 1978 title season, lead sponsor [[Imperial Tobacco]] pulled funding.<ref name="Hamlyn–1979" />{{rp||page=|pages=164}} In {{F1|1979}}, the team rolled out the [[Lotus 80]], whose downforce overwhelmed the car's suspension, generating [[porpoising]] issues, and whose weak chassis popped out rivets while driving.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=Lotus 80: No skirting around the problem |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/july-2004/136/lotus-80-no-skirting-around-the-problem/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194616/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/july-2004/136/lotus-80-no-skirting-around-the-problem/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti scored a podium in the Lotus 80's debut at [[1979 Spanish Grand Prix|Jarama]].<ref name="8W" /> His new teammate [[Carlos Reutemann]] refused to drive the car at all, and Andretti drove it only three times before returning to the Lotus 79, which was already out of date.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2021">{{Cite web |last=Malsher-Lopez |first=David |date=2021-03-30 |title=Carlos Reutemann - A Bright Star of F1 but Never its Champion |url=https://www.motorsport.com/f1/news/carlos-reutemann-ferrari-brabham-lotus/4774855/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=[[Motorsport.com]] |publisher=[[Motorsport Network]] |archive-date=February 25, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250225124012/https://www.motorsport.com/f1/news/carlos-reutemann-ferrari-brabham-lotus/4774855/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>Nye (1986) p. 100</ref> Andretti finished 12th in the standings, with 14 points, 6 points behind Reutemann,<ref>{{Cite web |title=1979 Driver Standings |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1979/drivers |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=December 27, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241227210542/https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1979/drivers |url-status=live }}</ref> who left for [[Williams Racing|Williams]] after the season.<ref name="Malsher-Lopez–2021" />

Following the failure of the Lotus 80, Chapman tried to solve the problem by developing the [[Lotus 88]], a complex and innovative carbon-fiber, dual-chassis structure.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Somerfield |first=Matt |date=2020-04-13 |title=Banned: Why Lotus' twin-chassis concept was outlawed |url=https://www.motorsport.com/f1/news/banned-tech-twin-chassis-lotus/4780030/#:~:text=Think%20then%20of%20the%20outer,sprung%20part%20of%20the%20car'. |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240410203126/https://www.motorsport.com/f1/news/banned-tech-twin-chassis-lotus/4780030/ |archive-date=2024-04-10 |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=[[Motorsport.com]] |publisher=[[Motorsport Network]]}}</ref> In theory, one chassis would absorb the porpoising while the other chassis would carry the driver.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Lotus 88 |url=https://www.f1technical.net/f1db/cars/473/lotus-88 |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=F1 Technical |archive-date=March 20, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250320045635/https://www.f1technical.net/f1db/cars/473/lotus-88 |url-status=live }}</ref> The team used a transitional car, the [[Lotus 81]], for {{F1|1980}}, while Chapman developed the 88. Lotus replaced Reutemann with two talented teammates, [[Elio de Angelis]] and (briefly) [[Nigel Mansell]], but the team was again unsuccessful.<ref>Nye (1986) p. 196</ref> Andretti scored only one point all season. Over the course of the season, he lost faith in the developing Lotus 88, declaring that Chapman "got bored and started going crazy with other things that were outside of the rules."<ref name="Blackstock–2025">{{Cite web |last=Blackstock |first=Elizabeth |date=2025-01-15 |title=Revealed: The unfortunate team swap that ended an F1 career |url=https://www.planetf1.com/features/mario-andretti-team-swap-f1-career-alfa-romeo-mclaren |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=PlanetF1 |language=en |archive-date=January 19, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250119005622/https://www.planetf1.com/features/mario-andretti-team-swap-f1-career-alfa-romeo-mclaren |url-status=live }}</ref> He left Lotus at the end of the season, shortly before Chapman was about to unveil the Lotus 88 for 1981. After his departure, the FIA banned the Lotus 88.<ref name="Blackstock–2025" />

=== Alfa Romeo (1981) ===
[[File:Andretti at 1981 Dutch Grand Prix.jpg|thumb|Andretti driving the [[Alfa Romeo 179|Alfa Romeo 179C]] at the [[1981 Dutch Grand Prix]].]]For the [[1981 Formula One season|1981]] season, Andretti signed a sponsorship deal with [[Marlboro]], whose advertising chief [[John Hogan (motorsport executive)|John Hogan]] gave him a choice between the two Marlboro-sponsored teams, [[Alfa Romeo (Formula One)|Alfa Romeo]] and [[McLaren]].<ref>{{Cite web |date=2022-10-25 |title='Life is good' for Mario Andretti after dream McLaren F1 drive |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/december-2022/16/life-is-good-for-mario-andretti-after-dream-mclaren-f1-drive/ |access-date=2025-04-25 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=March 21, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250321225635/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/december-2022/16/life-is-good-for-mario-andretti-after-dream-mclaren-f1-drive/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti picked the Italian team due to his friendship with one of their engineers<ref name="Blackstock–2025" /> and the higher salary on offer.<ref name="8W" /> Before the 1981 season, the FIA outlawed sliding sideskirts, which the Alfa Romeo design team had relied on to generate ground effect.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Tipo 179F "test car" (1981) |url=https://www.museoalfaromeo.com/en-us/storia/Pages/Tipo-179F-test-car.aspx |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Museo Alfa Romeo |archive-date=February 8, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250208000112/https://www.museoalfaromeo.com/en-us/storia/Pages/Tipo-179F-test-car.aspx |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti finished fourth on his debut at the [[1981 United States Grand Prix West|United States Grand Prix West]], but the team was otherwise uncompetitive.<ref name="Blackstock–2025" /><ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=74}} He finished 17th in the Drivers' Championship, with 3 points.<ref>{{Cite web |title=1981 Driver Standings |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1981/drivers |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=February 7, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250207114319/https://www.formula1.com/en/results/1981/drivers |url-status=live }}</ref> He left the team after the season, explaining that the new generation of Formula One cars required "toggle switch driving with no need for any kind of delicacy [...] it made leaving Formula One a lot easier than it would have been."<ref>{{cite book | last = Roebuck | first = Nigel | title = Grand Prix Greats | publisher = Patrick Stephens Ltd | year = 1986 | isbn = 0-85059-792-7 | page=24}}</ref>

=== Stand-in appearances (1982) ===
During the {{F1|1982}} season, Andretti briefly raced for both the Drivers' and Constructors' Championship-winning teams, [[Williams Racing|Williams]] and Ferrari. Andretti joined Williams for the [[1982 United States Grand Prix West|United States Grand Prix West]] after Reutemann abruptly quit. He damaged his suspension after contacting a wall and retired.<ref name="MSM–2014">{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=United States Grand Prix West race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/may-1982/46/united-stateswest-grand-prix/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=December 26, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241226081223/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/may-1982/46/united-stateswest-grand-prix/ |url-status=live }}</ref> IndyCar commitments prevented him from signing a full-time contract,<ref name="MSM–2014" /> and Williams' [[Keke Rosberg]] won the Drivers' Championship.<ref name="MSM–2014Vegas">{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1982 Las Vegas Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/november-1982/43/las-vegas-grand-prix/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194750/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/november-1982/43/las-vegas-grand-prix/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

Andretti then replaced the injured [[Didier Pironi]] at Ferrari for the last two races of the season. He took pole and finished third at the {{F1 GP|1982|Italian}}.<ref>{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=1982 Italian Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/october-1982/46/italian-grand-prix-6/ |access-date=2025-01-27 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=February 5, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250205101741/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/october-1982/46/italian-grand-prix-6/ |url-status=live }}</ref> At the season-ending {{F1 GP|1982|Caesars Palace}}, Andretti's final Formula One race, he retired with a suspension failure, but Niki Lauda's engine failure clinched the Constructors' Championship for Ferrari.<ref name="MSM–2014Vegas" /> Andretti agreed to serve as [[Renault]]'s reserve driver for one U.S. race in 1984,<ref>{{Cite web |last=Cain |first=Carol |date=1984-06-22 |title=Famed driver Mario Andretti may return to Formula One... |url=https://www.upi.com/Archives/1984/06/22/Famed-driver-Mario-Andretti-may-return-to-Formula-One/1454456724800/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=UPI |language=en}}</ref> but declined to be considered for a reserve role in 1986, effectively ending his Formula One career.<ref>{{cite web|title= America's Lost Generation|last= Glikin|first= Roy|date= February 12, 1997|website= Atlas F1 Magazine|url= http://atlasf1.autosport.com/97/winter2/glikin.html|access-date= March 6, 2021|archive-date= October 22, 2020|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20201022143903/http://atlasf1.autosport.com/97/winter2/glikin.html|url-status= live}}</ref>

== CART IndyCar career ==

=== Penske (1979–1980) ===
[[File:Penske PC-9 Indy Car, built for Mario Andretti, 1980 - Collings Foundation - Massachusetts - DSC07041.jpg|thumb|Andretti drove the [[Penske PC-9]] during the [[1980 CART season]].]]In 1979, a new organization, [[Championship Auto Racing Teams]] (CART), set up the IndyCar World Series,<ref>{{cite book | editor-last = Hamilton | editor-first = Maurice | title = Autocourse 1983 – 1984 | publisher = Hazleton Publishing | year = 1983 | isbn = 0-905138-25-2 |page=219 }}</ref> which displaced the USAC championship.<ref name="IndyCar split and reunification" /> CART was formed because the larger and more institutional IndyCar teams, like Andretti's [[Penske Racing]], wanted the sport to emphasize technical innovation (the costs of which deterred new entrants) and a more structured commercial strategy.<ref name="IndyCar split and reunification" /> After Penske helped start CART, Andretti sporadically competed in CART during the [[1989 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1979]] and [[1980 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1980]] seasons, winning one race at [[Michigan International Speedway|Michigan]] in 1980.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1979 CART Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1979/R/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=November 8, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231108013226/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1979/R/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1980 CART Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1980/R/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=April 12, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250412041810/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1980/R/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Patrick (1981–1982) ===
Andretti switched to [[Patrick Racing]] for the [[1981 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1981]] season. The move reunited him with [[STP Corporation]], the team's sponsor, and Jim McGee, Andretti's mechanic from DVL and Parnelli.<ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=76}} He did not win a race, but recorded five top-five finishes in seven races; the other two results were mechanical DNFs.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1981 CART Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1980/R/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=April 12, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250412041810/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1980/R/ |url-status=live }}</ref> At the [[1981 Indianapolis 500]], Andretti was controversially stripped of the win four months after the race.<ref name="Benbow–2019">{{Cite web |last=Benbow |first=Dana Hunsinger |title=Indy 500's most controversial finish: It ruined Mario Andretti, Bobby Unser friendship forever |url=https://www.indystar.com/story/sports/2019/05/06/indy-500-s-most-controversial-finish-ruined-mario-andretti-bobby-unser-friendship/1116614001/ |access-date=2025-01-28 |website=[[The Indianapolis Star]] |language=en-US |archive-date=March 22, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230322203742/https://www.indystar.com/story/sports/2019/05/06/indy-500-s-most-controversial-finish-ruined-mario-andretti-bobby-unser-friendship/1116614001/ |url-status=live }}</ref> After leaving Alfa Romeo, Andretti joined CART full-time for the [[1982 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1982]] season. He finished third in the season standings,<ref name="Mario Andretti Results" /> with six podiums in 11 races.<ref name="1982 CART Results">{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1982 CART Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1982/R/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=November 8, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231108221631/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1982/R/ |url-status=live }}</ref> As with 1981, all his other results were mechanical DNFs.<ref name="1982 CART Results" />

=== Newman/Haas (1983–1994) ===
In 1983, Andretti joined the new [[Newman/Haas Racing]] team, set up by [[Carl Haas]] and actor (and former [[Can-Am]] team owner) [[Paul Newman]].<ref name="Newman/Haas">{{Cite web |last=Kirby |first=Gordon |date=2014-07-07 |title=Newman/Haas: the final scene? |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/march-2012/106/newman-haas-the-final-scene/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194542/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/march-2012/106/newman-haas-the-final-scene/ |url-status=live }}</ref> The team used cars built by British company [[Lola Cars|Lola]], in contrast to the [[March Engineering|March]] cars in vogue at the time.<ref name="Newman/Haas's first championship" /> The team lured Andretti by promising to run only one car, making him the focus of the team.<ref name="Newman/Haas" /> Andretti spent the rest of his full-time racing career with Newman/Haas.<ref name="8W" />

==== Solo-racer era ====
In [[1983 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1983]], Andretti worked with the team to develop the uncompetitive [[Lola T700]] into a decent car.<ref name="Andretti's 1984 title">{{Cite web |last=Kirby |first=Gordon |date=2014-10-13 |title=Andretti's 1984 title |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/us-scene/indycar/andrettis-1984-title/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=December 9, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241209095403/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/us-scene/indycar/andrettis-1984-title/ |url-status=live }}</ref> At round six, he took the team's maiden win at [[Road America|Elkhart Lake]], and scored another win in Las Vegas. He recorded eight top-five finishes in 13 starts.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1983 CART Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1983/R/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=November 8, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231108012053/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1983/R/ |url-status=live }}</ref>[[File:Mario Andretti 1984.jpg|thumb|Andretti at the [[1984 Pocono 500]] in [[Long Pond, Pennsylvania]]]]In [[1984 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1984]], the team commissioned a new chassis, which became the [[Lola T800]]. The car was designed by Lotus veteran Nigel Bennett and effectively utilized the [[Ground effect (cars)|ground effect]] technology that Formula One had just banned in 1982.<ref name="Newman/Haas's first championship" /><ref name="GroundEffect2022">{{Cite web |date=2022-04-05 |title=Watch: The origins of ground effect |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/latest/article/watch-the-origins-of-ground-effect.6i1MZBs5xe6cjO2deb57hV |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Formula One |language=en |archive-date=March 20, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250320025645/https://www.formula1.com/en/latest/article/watch-the-origins-of-ground-effect.6i1MZBs5xe6cjO2deb57hV |url-status=live }}</ref> (Various CART teams had been attempting to develop ground effect cars since 1980 at the latest.<ref>{{Cite magazine |last=Moses |first=Sam |date=1980-05-19 |title=Three Vacuums Clean Up |url=https://vault.si.com/vault/1980/05/19/three-vacuums-clean-up-ground-effect-cars-driven-by-johnny-rutherford-mario-andretti-and-bobby-unser-who-grasped-their-black-magic-filled-the-front-row-for-indy |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230131105753/https://vault.si.com/vault/1980/05/19/three-vacuums-clean-up-ground-effect-cars-driven-by-johnny-rutherford-mario-andretti-and-bobby-unser-who-grasped-their-black-magic-filled-the-front-row-for-indy |archive-date=January 31, 2023 |access-date=2025-01-31 |magazine=Sports Illustrated |language=en-us |url-status=live }}</ref>) However, the team got off to a mediocre start. Andretti won the season opener at [[Grand Prix of Long Beach|Long Beach]], but his [[1984 Indianapolis 500|Indianapolis 500]] race was compromised by electrical issues, and his wheel fell off at the [[Milwaukee Mile]]. After four races, he trailed [[Tom Sneva]] by 58 points.<ref name="Andretti's 1984 title" /> In mid-season, however, he won five out of eight races,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1984 CART Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1984/R/ |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=November 4, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231104172041/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1984/R/ |url-status=live }}</ref> including the [[1984 Michigan 500|Michigan 500]], where he beat Sneva by 0.14 seconds, the closest finish in IndyCar history at the time.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Associated Press |date=1984-07-23 |title=Mario Andretti Wins |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1984/07/23/sports/mario-andretti-wins.html |access-date=2025-01-31 |work=The New York Times |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=April 12, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250412090841/https://www.nytimes.com/1984/07/23/sports/mario-andretti-wins.html |url-status=live }}</ref> After a tight, season-long battle, Andretti closed out the season with two conservative second-place drives, explaining that "I hated driving that way but that's what I had to do."<ref name="Andretti's 1984 title" /> He beat Sneva by 13 points to claim his fourth IndyCar title at the age of 44.<ref name="8W" /> At the end of the season, he was voted Driver of the Year for a third time.<ref name="Andretti's 1984 title" />

The team took a step back in [[1985 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1985]]. Other teams noticed that in addition to Andretti's six wins, [[Danny Sullivan]] won three races in a customer T800.<ref name="Newman/Haas's first championship" /> To make more money, Newman/Haas agreed to distribute the Lolas to more competitors, watering down its technical advantage.<ref>{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=1991-09-29 |title=Carl Haas: A Hands-On Owner |url=https://www.mcall.com/1991/09/29/carl-haas-a-hands-on-owner/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=[[The Morning Call]] |language=en-US}}</ref> Andretti got out to a fast start, winning three of the first four races and finishing second in the fourth, the [[1985 Indianapolis 500]]. After four races, he had a 34-point lead in the standings.<ref>{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=1985-06-30 |title=Andretti and Sullivan Resume Rivalry in Meadowlands Race |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1985-06-30-sp-268-story.html |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Los Angeles Times |language=en-US |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194639/https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1985-06-30-sp-268-story.html |url-status=live }}</ref> However, he recorded only one more top-five finish the rest of the way,<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1985 CART Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1985/R/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=November 8, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231108012819/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1985/R/ |url-status=live }}</ref> and finished fifth in the standings.<ref name="Racing–Reference" />

From 1986 to 1988, Andretti's son [[Michael Andretti|Michael]] emerged as a force in the sport. In [[1986 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1986]], Michael placed second, beating Mario for the first time. Father and son both scored five poles.<ref name="8W" /> At round five in Portland, Mario beat Michael by 0.07 seconds, setting another record for the closest finish in IndyCar history.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Glick |first=Shav |date=1987-10-11 |title=Indy Car Race at Laguna Seca : Mario Andretti Takes Pole and Makes a Point at the Expense of His Son |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1987-10-11-sp-13432-story.html |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Los Angeles Times |language=en-US |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194751/https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1987-10-11-sp-13432-story.html |url-status=live }}</ref> In addition, at age 46, he finally won his home race, the [[1986 Domino's Pizza 500|Pocono 500]], after 14 attempts.<ref>{{Cite news |last= |date=1986-08-18 |title=Pocono Easy Drive for Mario Andretti |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1986/08/18/sports/auto-racing-pocono-easy-drive-for-mario-andretti.html |access-date=2025-01-30 |work=The New York Times |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194524/https://www.nytimes.com/1986/08/18/sports/auto-racing-pocono-easy-drive-for-mario-andretti.html |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=2021-12-27 |title=50 Years of The Tricky Triangle - The 1980's |url=https://www.poconoraceway.com/50-years-of-the-tricky-triangle-1980s/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Pocono Raceway - The Tricky Triangle |language=en-US |archive-date=December 6, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20241206131105/https://www.poconoraceway.com/50-years-of-the-tricky-triangle-1980s/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He called it "one of the happiest weekends [he had] ever had."<ref name="newspapers.com">{{cite news |date=August 18, 1986 |title=At Last, Andretti Feels at Home in Pocono |url=https://www.newspapers.com/article/philadelphia-daily-news-1986-pocono-andr/130074080/ |work=Philadelphia Daily News |page=85 |agency=Associated Press |archive-date=August 16, 2023 |access-date=January 30, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230816002202/https://www.newspapers.com/article/philadelphia-daily-news-1986-pocono-andr/130074080/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He led the championship with ten races to go,<ref name="newspapers.com" /> but did not pick up another podium the rest of the way.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti: 1986 CART Results |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1986/R/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=November 8, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231108043456/https://www.racing-reference.info/driver-season-stats/andrema01/1986/R/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

In [[1987 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1987]], with an [[Adrian Newey]]-designed chassis and new engines designed by [[Ilmor]],<ref>{{Cite web |last=Pruett |first=Marshall |date=2017-05-17 |title=Rear View: Mario Andretti's 1987 Indy domination |url=https://racer.com/2017/05/17/rear-view-mario-andretti-s-1987-indy-domination/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=[[Racer (magazine)|RACER]] |language=en-US |archive-date=May 23, 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220523201922/https://racer.com/2017/05/17/rear-view-mario-andretti-s-1987-indy-domination/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti picked up eight poles but converted them into two wins.<ref name="8W" /> He dominated the [[1987 Indianapolis 500|Indianapolis 500]] but dropped out with a blown engine late in the race.<ref name="Newman/Haas" /> At the following race at Milwaukee, he passed [[A. J. Foyt]] for the all-time lead in career laps led. However, he crashed when his rear wing came loose and injured his neck. He called it "the hardest hit I've ever taken."<ref>{{Cite web |date=1987-06-01 |title=When Mario Andretti crashed on the 149th lap of... |url=https://www.upi.com/Archives/1987/06/01/When-Mario-Andretti-crashed-on-the-149th-lap-of/5473549518400/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=UPI |language=en |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194645/https://www.upi.com/Archives/1987/06/01/When-Mario-Andretti-crashed-on-the-149th-lap-of/5473549518400/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

In [[1988 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1988]], Andretti finished fifth in the season standings, one spot ahead of Michael.<ref name="8W" /> He picked up two wins, but continued to suffer from reliability issues and was involved in several costly accidents.<ref>{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=1988-09-18 |title=His Overall Record Doesn't Tell the Story of Mario's Season |url=https://www.mcall.com/1988/09/18/his-overall-record-doesnt-tell-story-of-marios-season/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=[[The Morning Call]] |language=en-US |archive-date=April 13, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250413153816/https://www.mcall.com/1988/09/18/his-overall-record-doesnt-tell-story-of-marios-season/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

==== Two-car era ====
Michael Andretti joined Newman/Haas in [[1989 CART PPG Indy Car World Series|1989]], which added a second car for the first time to accommodate him.<ref name="8W" /> Mario and Michael became the first father/son team to compete in both [[IMSA GT Championship|IMSA GT]] and IndyCar racing.<ref name="texaco" /> Michael reached the peak of his career, winning the 1991 championship and finishing second in 1990 and 1992. By contrast, Mario performed well but not brilliantly. During the 63 races from 1989 to 1992, he scored 30 top-five finishes but recorded no wins.<ref name="Racing–Reference" /><ref name="8W" /> In 1992, he set the all-time record for most IndyCar starts, passing A. J. Foyt.<ref>{{Cite news |date=1992-08-06 |title=Auto Racing: 370th Start for Andretti |url=https://www.nytimes.com/1992/08/06/sports/sports-people-auto-racing-370th-start-for-andretti.html |access-date=2025-01-31 |work=The New York Times |language=en-US |issn=0362-4331 |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194625/https://www.nytimes.com/1992/08/06/sports/sports-people-auto-racing-370th-start-for-andretti.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

Ahead of the [[1993 PPG Indy Car World Series|1993]] season, Michael Andretti left CART for Formula One.<ref>{{Cite web |last=O'Keefe |first=Thomas |date=2000 |title=Man for One Season |url=https://atlasf1.autosport.com/2000/ita/okeefe.html |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Atlas F1 Magazine |archive-date=August 17, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250817074128/http://atlasf1.autosport.com/2000/ita/okeefe.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Mario wanted to return to the old one-car system, but the team replaced Michael with the reigning Formula One champion, [[Nigel Mansell]], and gave Mansell number one driver status. Mansell and Andretti raced as teammates for two years, but did not get along, owing to their mutual competitiveness and personality differences.<ref name="Andretti vs Mansell">{{Cite web |last=Lerner |first=Preston |date=2023-04-03 |title=Andretti vs Mansell: the 1993 IndyCar team-mates who couldn't get along |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/may-2023/100/andretti-vs-mansell-the-1993-indycar-team-mates-who-couldnt-get-along/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=May 19, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250519055937/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/may-2023/100/andretti-vs-mansell-the-1993-indycar-team-mates-who-couldnt-get-along/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti scored his last IndyCar win during the 1993 [[Phoenix Raceway|Phoenix]] race.<ref name="NYTimes04051993" /> At 53 years and 34 days old, he became the oldest recorded winner of an IndyCar event.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Harris |first=Mike |date=1993-04-11 |title=Andretti's Win One for the Ages |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1993-04-11-sp-21653-story.html |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Los Angeles Times |language=en-US |archive-date=January 24, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250124031727/https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1993-04-11-sp-21653-story.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Later that year, he qualified on pole at the [[1993 Michigan 500|Michigan 500]] with a speed of {{convert|234.275|mph|km/h}}, setting a new closed-course world record.<ref name="O'Leary–2002" />{{rp||page=104}} He finished sixth in the season standings, while Mansell won the title.<ref>{{Cite web |title=CART Standings for 1993 |url=https://www.racing-reference.info/yeardet/1993/R |access-date=2025-01-30 |work=Racing-Reference |publisher=NASCAR Digital Media, LLC |language=en-US |archive-date=May 26, 2024 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240526215541/https://www.racing-reference.info/yeardet/1993/R |url-status=live }}</ref>

Andretti decided to race one final season, dubbed "The Arrivederci Tour." In [[1994 PPG Indy Car World Series|1994]], the team as a whole took a step back, and Newman/Haas went winless for the first time. At his 407th, and final, IndyCar race, at [[Laguna Seca]], Andretti's race was initially derailed by a flat tire, but he weaved his way back up to seventh. His engine failed with four laps to go.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Lerner |first=Preston |date=2023-04-03 |title=Andretti vs Mansell: the 1993 IndyCar team-mates who couldn't get along |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/may-2023/100/andretti-vs-mansell-the-1993-indycar-team-mates-who-couldnt-get-along/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=May 19, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250519055937/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/may-2023/100/andretti-vs-mansell-the-1993-indycar-team-mates-who-couldnt-get-along/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="8W" /> At the time of his retirement, his 52 wins were the second-most in history, behind only A. J. Foyt's 67. ([[Scott Dixon]] passed him in 2022.)<ref>{{Cite web |last=Ryan |first=Nate |date=2022-08-07 |title=Scott Dixon wins in wild Nashville comeback to pass Mario Andretti on IndyCar win list |url=https://www.nbcsports.com/motor-sports/news/scott-dixon-win-wild-comeback-nashville-mario-andretti-indycar-list-mario-andretti |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=NBC Sports |language=en-US |archive-date=February 13, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250213010056/https://www.nbcsports.com/motor-sports/news/scott-dixon-win-wild-comeback-nashville-mario-andretti-indycar-list-mario-andretti |url-status=live }}</ref> His 7,595 laps led remain the all-time record, nearly 1,000 laps higher than second-placed Michael Andretti's 6,692.<ref name="Pryson–2022" /> His 67 pole positions were the all-time record. ([[Will Power]] passed him in 2022.)<ref>{{Cite web |last=Fryer |first=Jenna |date=2022-09-10 |title=Power breaks Andretti qualifying record in pursuit of title |url=https://apnews.com/article/sports-indycar-mario-andretti-monterey-will-power-a81759ea3ca19bba7e14d20dbe5eb4e5 |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=AP News |language=en |archive-date=November 25, 2023 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231125201216/https://apnews.com/article/sports-indycar-mario-andretti-monterey-will-power-a81759ea3ca19bba7e14d20dbe5eb4e5 |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Indianapolis 500 ===
{{Main|Andretti curse}}
Andretti won once at the [[Indianapolis 500]] in 29 attempts, despite three pole positions and seven top-three grid placements.<ref name="Indianapolis 500 Career" /> He finished all {{convert|500|mi|km}} just five times,<ref name="Indianapolis 500 Career" /> and quipped that "if it had been the Indy 400, I'd have had at least six."<ref name="Roebuck–2000" /> He had so many incidents and near victories at the track that critics have suggested the existence of an "Andretti Curse."<ref name="KnightRidder">{{cite web|title=What curse? Andretti is back at Indy |work=[[Knight Ridder]] |author=Steve Crowe |url=http://archive.southcoasttoday.com/daily/05-01/05-26-01/c05sp125.htm |access-date=July 11, 2007 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071020095644/http://archive.southcoasttoday.com/daily/05-01/05-26-01/c05sp125.htm |archive-date=October 20, 2007 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web|title='I didn't miss this': Andretti survives difficult weekend to get in field |date=May 14, 2001 |work=[[CNN]]/[[Sports Illustrated]] |url=http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/motorsports/2001/indy500/news/2001/05/14/andretti_difficulty_ap/ |access-date=July 11, 2007 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040608163940/http://sportsillustrated.cnn.com/motorsports/2001/indy500/news/2001/05/14/andretti_difficulty_ap/ |archive-date=June 8, 2004 }}</ref>

Andretti occasionally did well at Indianapolis. He won the [[1969 Indianapolis 500|1969 race]], but benefited from good luck: he completed the race in the team's backup car, a now-outdated [[Brawner Hawk]], and on just one set of tires. His race engineer said that the Hawk's gearbox was failing and would not have lasted another five laps.<ref name="Kravitz–2021" /> He was also the first driver to exceed {{convert|200|mi/h|km/h}}, during practice for the [[1977 Indianapolis 500|1977 race]].<ref name="ESPNclassic" />

Starting in 1981, Andretti encountered several out-of-the-ordinary instances of bad luck at the Indianapolis 500. In [[1981 Indianapolis 500|1981]], he lost after [[Bobby Unser]] passed cars under caution.<ref name="Benbow–2019" /> In [[1985 Indianapolis 500|1985]], he finished second to [[Danny Sullivan]], who miraculously spun without crashing.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Horrall |first=Zach |date=2020-05-01 |title=Sullivan's Miraculous 'Spin and Win' Will Live Forever in Indianapolis 500 Lore |url=https://www.indianapolismotorspeedway.com/news-multimedia/news/2020/05/01/danny-sullivan-spin-and-win-lives-on-in-indy-500-lore |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=Indianapolis Motor Speedway |language=en |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194624/https://www.indianapolismotorspeedway.com/news-multimedia/news/2020/05/01/danny-sullivan-spin-and-win-lives-on-in-indy-500-lore |url-status=live }}</ref> In [[1987 Indianapolis 500|1987]], he led 170 of the first 177 laps but slowed down to preserve his engine, which ironically caused the engine to fail.<ref>{{cite web|title=Greatest 33 Profile: Mario Andretti |url=http://www.indianapolismotorspeedway.com/history/51458/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140701233441/http://www.indianapolismotorspeedway.com/history/51458/ |archive-date=July 1, 2014|publisher=Indianapolis Motor Speedway|access-date=July 30, 2021 }}</ref> In [[1992 Indianapolis 500|1992]], he broke six toes, his son Jeff broke both legs, and his son Michael lost a 28-second lead with 12 laps to go due to a mechanical failure.<ref name="Andretti book">{{cite book |last=Andretti |first=Mario |author-link=Mario Andretti |title=Andretti |date=1994 |publisher=Collins Publishers San Francisco |isbn=978-0006383024 |pages=45}}</ref> Finally, in his last serious chance at a win in [[1993 Indianapolis 500|1993]], he led the most laps, but his race was derailed after the team incorrectly changed the tire stagger on his car during a late pit stop.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Glick |first=Shav |date=1993-05-31 |title=Indianapolis 500: Everything Goes Green for Fittipaldi  |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-1993-05-31-sp-41875-story.html |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=Los Angeles Times |language=en-US}}</ref><ref name="Andretti vs Mansell" /> In addition, in [[2003 Indianapolis 500|2003]], the 63-year-old Andretti tested the injured [[Tony Kanaan]]'s car at Indianapolis but got into a "spectacular" airborne crash when [[Kenny Bräck]] crashed in front of him; he escaped with minor injuries.<ref name="8W" /> Reflecting on the curse in 2019, Andretti said that while he "think[s] about all the times [he] should have won here," he also won in 1969, "when everything went wrong."<ref name="Kravitz–2021" />

== Sportscar racing career ==

=== North American endurance racing ===
Andretti's first race in a sportscar was in 1965, when he piloted a [[Ferrari 275 P]] at the Bridgehampton 500 km at [[Bridgehampton Race Circuit|Bridgehampton]]; he did not finish.<ref name="RSC">{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti (USA) - All Results |url=https://www.racingsportscars.com/driver/results/Mario-Andretti-USA.html |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=Racing Sports Cars |archive-date=October 31, 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191031232451/https://www.racingsportscars.com/driver/results/Mario-Andretti-USA.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti won three [[12 Hours of Sebring]] endurance races (1967, 1970, 1972),<ref name="sportscentury" /> and a [[1972 World Sportscar Championship|6-hour race at Daytona]] in 1972.<ref name="Daytona6Hours">{{Cite web |title=When 24 = 6: The Story Behind the 1972 Daytona "24 hours" |url=https://www.racingarchives.org/blogpost/when-24-6-the-story-behind-the-1972-daytona-24-hours/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=International Motor Racing Research Center |archive-date=June 26, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250626191721/https://www.racingarchives.org/blogpost/when-24-6-the-story-behind-the-1972-daytona-24-hours/ |url-status=live }}</ref> In early sportscar races he competed for [[Holman-Moody]], but later often drove for Ferrari.<ref name="RSC" />

Andretti signed with Ferrari in 1971, and won several races with co-driver [[Jacky Ickx]].<ref name="FormulaOneBio" /> In [[1972 World Sportscar Championship|1972]], he shared wins in the three North American rounds of the championship and at [[Brands Hatch]] in the UK, helping Ferrari to a dominant victory in that year's World Championship for Makes.<ref name="Nye–2017">{{Cite web |last=Nye |first=Doug |date=2017-02-23 |title=Remembering the dominance of the Ferrari 312 PB |url=https://www.goodwood.com/grr/columnists/doug-nye/doug-nye-remembering-the-dominance-of-the-ferrari-312pb/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Goodwood Road & Racing |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 30, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250130202705/https://www.goodwood.com/grr/columnists/doug-nye/doug-nye-remembering-the-dominance-of-the-ferrari-312pb/ |url-status=live }}</ref> He also competed in 25 North American [[Can-Am]] races in the late 1960s and early 1970s, with a best finish of third place at [[Riverside International Raceway|Riverside]] in [[1969 Can-Am season|1969]].<ref name="RSC" />

=== Le Mans ===
Andretti competed at the [[24 Hours of Le Mans]] in four decades. In [[1966 24 Hours of Le Mans|1966]], he shared a Holman-Moody [[Ford GT40|Ford Mk II]] with [[Lucien Bianchi]]. They retired due to valve failure.<ref>{{cite web | title=Le Mans 1966 | work=GT40.org | url=http://www.gt40.org.uk/Le%20Mans/1966.htm | access-date=June 15, 2007 | archive-date=October 22, 2007 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071022112522/http://gt40.org.uk/Le%20Mans/1966.htm | url-status=dead }}</ref> In [[1967 24 Hours of Le Mans|1967]], during a 3:30 am pit stop, a mechanic accidentally installed a front brake pad backwards, causing Andretti's brakes to lock up at the Dunlop Bridge. He crashed, broke several ribs,<ref name="1967 24h Le Mans crash">{{Cite web |date=2014-07-07 |title=The dream isn't over |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/june-2006/39/the-dream-isnt-over/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB |archive-date=January 29, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250129182445/https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/june-2006/39/the-dream-isnt-over/ |url-status=live }}</ref> and was left exposed to oncoming traffic, but [[Roger McCluskey]] pulled him to safety.<ref>{{cite web|title=Ford Again at Le Mans |work=[[Autosport]] |year=1967 |author=Gregor Grant |url=http://www.imca-slotracing.com/1967-PART2.htm |access-date=June 15, 2007 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070928142539/http://www.imca-slotracing.com/1967-PART2.htm |archive-date=September 28, 2007 }}</ref><ref>{{cite web | title=Le Mans 1967 | work=GT40.org | url=http://www.gt40.org.uk/Le%20Mans/1967.htm | access-date=June 15, 2007 | archive-date=October 22, 2007 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071022112528/http://gt40.org.uk/Le%20Mans/1967.htm | url-status=dead }}</ref>[[File:1989Porsche962MarioAndrettiMichaelAndrettiMillerHighLife.jpg|thumb|Andretti's 1988 [[Porsche 962]], co-driven with son [[Michael Andretti|Michael]].]]Andretti did not return to Le Mans until ending his full-time Formula One career. In {{24hLM|1982}}, he partnered with son Michael in a Mirage M12 Ford. They qualified in ninth place, but although their car passed initial inspection several days earlier,<ref name="ian">{{cite book |last=Briggs |first=Ian |title=Endurance Racing 1981–1991 |publisher=Osprey Automotive |year=1991 |isbn=1-85532-228-5}}</ref> it was disqualified shortly before the race started due to an improper oil cooler.<ref name="1967 24h Le Mans crash" /> They returned the [[1983 24 Hours of Le Mans|following year]] and finished third in a Porsche customer car, behind two works Porsches.<ref name="8W" /> The Andrettis returned in {{24hLM|1988}} with Mario's nephew [[John Andretti|John]] added to the family team. Although they obtained a factory [[Porsche 962]], one of the car's engine cylinders failed,<ref name="1967 24h Le Mans crash" /> and the team finished fifth.<ref name="8W" />

Following Andretti's retirement from full-time racing, he decided to try for another Le Mans victory, joining [[Courage Compétition]] from 1995 to 1997. In {{24hLM|1995}}, the team qualified third, but Andretti was brake-checked by the car in front of him and crashed, forcing him to pit and costing the team six laps. The team eventually rallied from 25th to second in the overall classification,<ref name="8W" /> and finished first in the [[Le Mans Prototype|LMP1]] class.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Le Mans 24 Hours 1995 - Race Results |url=https://www.racingsportscars.com/results/Le_Mans-1995-06-18.html |access-date=2025-01-31 |website=www.racingsportscars.com |archive-date=August 28, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210828211228/https://www.racingsportscars.com/results/Le_Mans-1995-06-18.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Andretti later said that the team "lost [the 1995] race five times over" through poor organization, including a botched pit stop, an ill-considered switch to wet-weather tires, and a two-minute pit stop to wash the car to clean up the sponsor decals.<ref name="1967 24h Le Mans crash" /> Porsche withdrew active support from Courage in {{24hLM|1996}},<ref name="1967 24h Le Mans crash" /> and the team finished 16th after losing 90 minutes in the pits fixing an electronic issue and a broken axle.<ref name="8W" /> In {{24hLM|1997}}, the "now ancient Courage" was a backmarker and the team did not finish the race.<ref name="8W" /> Andretti's final appearance at Le Mans was at the [[2000 24 Hours of Le Mans|2000 race]], six years after his retirement from full-time racing. The 60-year-old Andretti drove the [[Panoz]] LMP-1 Roadster-S to a 15th-place finish.<ref>{{Cite journal  | last = Watkins  | first = Gary  | title = The dream isn't over  | journal = Motorsport  | volume = LXXXII| issue   =6 | pages = 39–42  |date=June 2006 }}</ref>

== Awards and honors ==
=== Legacy ===
[[File:Passion, dedication, responsibility; Racing legend tells how military, motor racing share mindset 110121-F-EJ686-367.jpg|thumb|right|Andretti has regularly driven the two seater IndyCar before races since his retirement.]]

Over the course of his long career, Andretti won over 100 races on major circuits, although the exact numbers vary depending on the definition of a major circuit. The [[International Motorsports Hall of Fame]] puts the total at either 109 or 111,<ref name="IMHoF" /><ref>{{Cite web |last= |first= |date=2022-11-04 |title=5 Fun Facts About Mario Andretti |url=https://www.motorsportshalloffame.com/5-fun-facts-about-mario-andretti/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=International Motorsports Hall of Fame |language=en-US |archive-date=February 8, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250208200801/https://www.motorsportshalloffame.com/5-fun-facts-about-mario-andretti/ |url-status=live }}</ref> while Andretti and the [[Automotive Hall of Fame]] put the total at 111.<ref name="Career Highlights">{{Cite web |last=Andretti |first=Mario |title=Career Highlights |url=https://www.marioandretti.com/career-highlights |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=MarioAndretti.com |archive-date=March 18, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250318134939/https://www.marioandretti.com/career-highlights |url-status=live }}</ref><ref name="AHOF–2025">{{Cite web |title=Mario Andretti |url=https://www.automotivehalloffame.org/honoree/mario-andretti/ |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=Automotive Hall of Fame |archive-date=February 12, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250212210755/https://www.automotivehalloffame.org/honoree/mario-andretti/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

Andretti's name has become synonymous with speed in American popular culture.<ref name="C16">{{cite web |date=May 22, 2007 |title=Mario Andretti: Living Legend (an interview) |url=http://www.c16mag.com/pacenote_item.aspx?cid=1410 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070914025720/http://www.c16mag.com/pacenote_item.aspx?cid=1410 |archive-date=September 14, 2007 |access-date=June 14, 2007 |work=C16 Magazine}}</ref> An extremely versatile driver, Andretti stands alone, or close to it, in several lists of drivers to win in multiple categories:

* Only driver to win the [[Indianapolis 500]] ([[1969 Indianapolis 500|1969]]), [[Daytona 500]] ([[1967 Daytona 500|1967]]), and the [[Formula One World Drivers' Championship]] ([[1978 Formula One season|1978]]) (as of 2025).<ref>{{Cite web |last=Martin |first=Bruce |date=2025-01-16 |title=Mario Andretti: Career 'richer' for battles with A.J. Foyt |url=https://www.indycar.com/News/2015/01/1-16-Mario-Andretti-talks-about-AJ-Foyt |access-date=2025-01-29 |website=IndyCar |archive-date=April 11, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250411194541/https://www.indycar.com/News/2015/01/1-16-Mario-Andretti-talks-about-AJ-Foyt |url-status=live }}</ref>
* One of only two drivers (including [[Dan Gurney]]) to have won races in [[Formula One]], [[American open-wheel car racing|IndyCar]], the [[World Sportscar Championship]], and [[NASCAR]] (as of 2019).<ref>{{Cite web |last=Brown |first=Maury |date=2019-05-14 |title=An Interview With Mario Andretti, Auto Racing's Biggest Icon |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/maurybrown/2019/05/10/mario-andretti-remains-racings-biggest-icon/ |access-date=2025-01-30 |website=Forbes |language=en |archive-date=April 8, 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250408130300/https://www.forbes.com/sites/maurybrown/2019/05/10/mario-andretti-remains-racings-biggest-icon/ |url-status=live }}</ref>
* One of only three drivers to have won major races on [[Road racing|road courses]], paved [[oval track|oval]]s, and [[dirt track racing|dirt tracks]] in one season, a feat that he accomplished four times (as of 2007).<ref name="sportscentury" />

With his final IndyCar win in April 1993, Andretti became the first driver to have won IndyCar races in four different decades<ref name="NYTimes04051993">{{cite web | title=Andretti Races to Victory | work=[[New York Times]] | date= April 5, 1993 | url=https://query.nytimes.com/gst/fullpage.html?res=9F0CE7DB133EF936A35757C0A965958260 | access-date=September 26, 2007 }}</ref><ref name="8W" /> and the first to win automobile races of any kind in five.<ref name="sportscentury">{{cite web |author=Schwartz |first=Larry |title=Super Mario had speed to burn |url=https://www.espn.com/classic/biography/s/andretti_mario.html |access-date=June 14, 2007 |work=[[ESPN]] |archive-date=April 11, 2016 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160411050910/http://espn.go.com/classic/biography/s/Andretti_Mario.html |url-status=live }}</ref> As of 2024, Andretti's victory at the [[1978 Dutch Grand Prix]] is the most recent Formula One win by an American driver.<ref>{{cite web |author=Dave Kallmann |date=June 18, 2005 |title=U.S. Grand Prix; Feel the need for Speed; Formula One racer tops |url=http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_qn4196/is_20050618/ai_n14672796 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130726114333/http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_qn4196/is_20050618/ai_n14672796 |archive-date=July 26, 2013 |access-date=June 12, 2007 |work=[[Milwaukee Journal Sentinel]]}}</ref>
